Tuthill had spent years researching concert hall design and laid out the interior specifically to favor sound — fan-shaped, plaster-walled forms that, by accident as much as engineering, produced the acoustics that have defined the building's reputation ever since. Tchaikovsky conducted at the opening night gala.

In the years since, virtually every major classical musician has played here, and most of the major figures of twentieth-century popular music as well — the Beatles in 1964, Bob Dylan, Billie Holiday, Frank Sinatra, Ella Fitzgerald, Judy Garland. The American debuts of Strauss, Mahler, Dvořák, and Rachmaninoff all happened in this building. In 1960, Carnegie Hall was nearly demolished: the New York Philharmonic had decided to move to the new Lincoln Center complex, and the developer who owned Carnegie Hall planned to tear it down for an office tower. A coalition of musicians led by the violinist Isaac Stern saved the building at the very last possible moment — the city bought it in 1960 and a nonprofit was created to run it. The Philharmonic moved to Lincoln Center anyway. Carnegie Hall has remained, and the new concert hall they moved to has been extensively rebuilt twice since.
tours.shortDescription: Tchaikovsky inaugurated it in 1891; Isaac Stern saved it from the wrecking ball in 1960. The most famous concert hall in America, still running three rooms a night.

tours.title: The Cloisters

stops.title: The Cloisters
stops.caption: Start at the steps at the museum entrance looking west across the Hudson to the Palisades.
stops.transcriptText:
Rockefeller bought the view too.

At the northern tip of Manhattan, in Fort Tryon Park, stands The Cloisters — the medieval art branch of the Metropolitan Museum. From the steps at the entrance, you can see across the Hudson River to the cliffs of New Jersey, called the Palisades. The view from inside the museum, from the windows and terraces, is the same: the river, the cliffs, the forested park around you. It is one of the few places in New York City where you can look at a landscape that is essentially unchanged from the seventeenth century.

The Cloisters was built between 1934 and 1938, funded entirely by John D. Rockefeller Jr. Rockefeller had purchased the medieval art collection of the sculptor George Grey Barnard, who had been collecting fragments of dismantled European monasteries for decades. Rockefeller donated the collection to the Met and commissioned the architect Charles Collens to design a building to house it. The result is a museum that looks, deliberately, like a medieval monastery — but is built partly from real medieval monasteries. Stones, columns, arches, and entire sections of cloisters from twelfth-century French abbeys were shipped to New York and incorporated into the new building.

The four cloisters that give the museum its name are largely reassembled from those original pieces — the Cuxa, Saint-Guilhem, Bonnefont, and Trie cloisters, each from a different French abbey. Among them, the museum displays one of the most important medieval art collections in the Western Hemisphere, including the Unicorn Tapestries — seven late fifteenth-century tapestries that are among the most studied artworks in any museum.

[beat]

The view is the other thing Rockefeller bought. To preserve the medieval atmosphere from inside the museum, he purchased seven hundred acres of land across the Hudson River, in New Jersey, and donated it to the Palisades Interstate Park Commission to keep it from being developed. The cliffs you see from the museum windows have stayed undeveloped because Rockefeller paid to make sure they would.

Find a spot at the wall. Look across the river. What you're seeing is not nature. It is a view that was deliberately curated, and paid for, almost a hundred years ago.

tours.longDescription:
At the northern tip of Manhattan, in Fort Tryon Park, stands The Cloisters — the medieval art branch of the Metropolitan Museum. From the steps at the entrance you can see across the Hudson River to the cliffs of New Jersey, called the Palisades. The view from inside the museum is the same: the river, the cliffs, the forested park around you. It is one of the few places in New York City where you can look at a landscape essentially unchanged from the seventeenth century. The Cloisters was built between 1934 and 1938, funded entirely by John D. Rockefeller Jr., who had purchased the medieval art collection of the sculptor George Grey Barnard — fragments of dismantled European monasteries Barnard had been gathering for decades. Rockefeller donated the collection to the Met and commissioned the architect Charles Collens to design a building to house it.

The result is a museum that looks, deliberately, like a medieval monastery — and is built partly from real ones. Stones, columns, arches, and entire sections of cloisters from twelfth-century French abbeys were shipped to New York and incorporated into the new building. The four cloisters that give the museum its name are largely reassembled from those original pieces — Cuxa, Saint-Guilhem, Bonnefont, and Trie, each from a different French abbey. Among them the museum displays one of the most important medieval art collections in the Western Hemisphere, including the Unicorn Tapestries. The view is the other thing Rockefeller bought: to preserve the medieval atmosphere from inside, he purchased seven hundred acres of land across the Hudson River in New Jersey and donated it to the Palisades Interstate Park Commission to keep it from being developed. The cliffs you see from the museum windows have stayed undeveloped because Rockefeller paid to make sure they would.
tours.shortDescription: The Met's medieval art outpost in Fort Tryon Park, built 1934–38 from real twelfth-century French monasteries — and the cliffs across the Hudson are part of the view by design.

tours.title: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts

stops.title: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts
stops.caption: Stand at Josie Robertson Plaza by the Revson Fountain, Met Opera to the west.
stops.transcriptText:
Lincoln Center was built on a neighborhood that was demolished.

The plaza in front of you is the Josie Robertson Plaza, the central courtyard of Lincoln Center. At the center, the round basin of the Revson Fountain. Around it, three buildings: to your west, the Metropolitan Opera House, with its five tall arched windows. To your north, the David Geffen Hall — home of the New York Philharmonic. To your south, the David H. Koch Theater — home of New York City Ballet. Behind those buildings, Juilliard, the Chamber Music Society, several smaller theaters, and a film center. Together, they make up the largest performing arts complex in the world.

Lincoln Center opened in stages, between 1962 and 1969. The project was led by John D. Rockefeller the Third — the same Rockefeller family that built The Cloisters and Rockefeller Center. Three different architects designed the three main buildings: Wallace Harrison did the Met Opera, Max Abramovitz did the Philharmonic hall, Philip Johnson did the ballet theater. The plaza itself was designed to feel like an Italian piazza.

Before any of this was here, the site was a working-class neighborhood called San Juan Hill. It was one of the largest Black and Puerto Rican neighborhoods in Manhattan, home to about seven thousand people. The conditions were poor, but the community was tightly knit, and the music coming out of it — early jazz, Latin music — was nationally influential. In the late 1950s, the city designated the neighborhood as a "slum clearance" site under urban renewal laws, condemned the buildings, and evicted the residents. The neighborhood was demolished to make way for what you're standing on now.

[beat]

The most famous image of San Juan Hill is from a movie. The 1961 film of West Side Story was filmed in the half-demolished streets of the neighborhood, in the months just before the bulldozers finished the job. The neighborhood you see in the opening scene — the dance numbers, the playground basketball, the brick tenements — was being destroyed in real time as the cameras rolled. By the time the film opened in theaters, San Juan Hill no longer existed.

The Revson Fountain at the center pulses on a five-minute cycle. The complex is free to enter and walk through, whether there's a performance or not.

tours.longDescription:
The plaza is the Josie Robertson Plaza, the central courtyard of Lincoln Center, with the Revson Fountain at the center. Around it are three buildings: the Metropolitan Opera House to the west with its five tall arched windows, David Geffen Hall to the north (home of the New York Philharmonic), and the David H. Koch Theater to the south (home of New York City Ballet). Behind those are Juilliard, the Chamber Music Society, several smaller theaters, and a film center. Together they make up the largest performing arts complex in the world. Lincoln Center opened in stages between 1962 and 1969, led by John D. Rockefeller III. Three different architects designed the three main buildings — Wallace Harrison did the Met Opera, Max Abramovitz did the Philharmonic hall, Philip Johnson did the ballet theater. The plaza itself was designed to feel like an Italian piazza.

Before any of this was here, the site was a working-class neighborhood called San Juan Hill — one of the largest Black and Puerto Rican neighborhoods in Manhattan, home to about seven thousand people. The conditions were poor but the community was tightly knit, and the music coming out of it — early jazz, Latin music — was nationally influential. In the late 1950s the city designated the neighborhood as a slum-clearance site under urban renewal laws, condemned the buildings, and evicted the residents. The neighborhood was demolished to make way for what you're standing on. The most famous image of San Juan Hill is from a movie: the 1961 film of West Side Story was shot in the half-demolished streets in the months just before the bulldozers finished the job. By the time the film opened, San Juan Hill no longer existed. The Revson Fountain at the center pulses on a five-minute cycle. The complex is free to enter and walk through, whether there's a performance or not.
tours.shortDescription: The world's largest performing arts complex — Met Opera, Philharmonic, City Ballet, Juilliard — built 1962–69 on the demolished San Juan Hill neighborhood.

tours.title: Stonewall Inn

stops.title: Stonewall Inn, Greenwich Village
stops.caption: Stand at 53 Christopher Street looking at the red brick building with the rainbow flag.
stops.transcriptText:
The bar in front of you is a national monument.

Find the red brick building with the small rainbow flag in the front window. That's the Stonewall Inn, 53 Christopher Street, in the West Village. The building is two stories, narrower than its neighbors, with a faded red awning. It doesn't look like much. Across the street, Christopher Park — a small triangular park with a statue group called "Gay Liberation" by the sculptor George Segal: two pairs of figures, painted white. Around you, the West Village stretches in every direction: narrow streets, low brick townhouses, a neighborhood that has refused to fit the Manhattan grid.

On the night of June 28, 1969, the New York Police Department raided the Stonewall Inn. Police raids of gay bars were routine at the time — it was illegal to dance with a same-sex partner in New York, illegal to serve alcohol to gay patrons, illegal in many places to be openly gay. What was different that night was the response. The patrons did not disperse. They fought back. The crowd outside the bar grew, then grew larger, then grew larger still. The protests continued for six days.

The Stonewall Riots are now considered the founding moment of the modern gay rights movement. The first Pride March, one year later on June 28, 1970, started from this corner and walked uptown to Central Park. Every Pride parade since — in any city, anywhere in the world — traces back to that night and this bar.

In 2016, President Obama designated the Stonewall Inn and the surrounding seven and a half acres a national monument — the first in the United States dedicated to the LGBTQ+ rights movement.

[beat]

The bar is still operating. It's still a gay bar, though now it's also a destination — visitors come from around the world to see the place where it happened. On Pride weekend, the streets outside fill with hundreds of thousands of people.

The Village around you was what surrounded the bar in 1969 — a neighborhood that, since the early twentieth century, has been the city's center of bohemian, artistic, and queer life. Henry James, Edna St. Vincent Millay, Bob Dylan, James Baldwin, Patti Smith, Andy Warhol all lived or worked within a few blocks of where you're standing.

Walk Christopher Street. Cross into the park to see the Segal sculpture. The bar will be open later if you want to step inside.

tours.longDescription:
The red brick building with the small rainbow flag in the front window is the Stonewall Inn, 53 Christopher Street, in the West Village — two stories, narrower than its neighbors, with a faded red awning. It doesn't look like much. Across the street is Christopher Park, a small triangular park with George Segal's sculpture group Gay Liberation: two pairs of figures painted white. Around you the West Village stretches in every direction — narrow streets, low brick townhouses, a neighborhood that has refused to fit the Manhattan grid. On the night of June 28, 1969, the New York Police Department raided the Stonewall Inn. Police raids of gay bars were routine at the time — it was illegal to dance with a same-sex partner in New York, illegal to serve alcohol to gay patrons, illegal in many places to be openly gay. What was different that night was the response: the patrons did not disperse. They fought back. The crowd outside the bar grew, then grew larger, then grew larger still. The protests continued for six days.

The Stonewall Riots are now considered the founding moment of the modern gay rights movement. The first Pride March, one year later on June 28, 1970, started from this corner and walked uptown to Central Park. Every Pride parade since — in any city, anywhere in the world — traces back to that night and this bar. In 2016, President Obama designated the Stonewall Inn and the surrounding seven and a half acres a national monument, the first in the United States dedicated to the LGBTQ+ rights movement. The bar is still operating, and it's still a gay bar, though now it's also a destination — visitors come from around the world to see the place where it happened. The Village around you was what surrounded the bar in 1969: a neighborhood that, since the early twentieth century, has been the city's center of bohemian, artistic, and queer life — Henry James, Edna St. Vincent Millay, Bob Dylan, James Baldwin, Patti Smith, Andy Warhol all lived or worked within a few blocks.
tours.shortDescription: 53 Christopher Street — the West Village bar where, on June 28, 1969, patrons fought back against a police raid. First U.S. national monument to the LGBTQ+ rights movement.

tours.title: Bolhão Market

stops.title: Bolhão Market
stops.caption: Enter from Rua Formosa to the south and stop at the edge of the ground floor before moving through.
stops.transcriptText:
Before this was a market, before it was even a square, it was waterlogged ground with a stream running through it — and where the stream pooled, it formed a bubble, which in Portuguese is bolhão, and that is how a food market in the centre of Porto came to be named after a marsh that no longer exists.
The city council built on the site in eighteen thirty-nine, but the building you're standing in front of now came seventy-five years after that, in nineteen fourteen, designed by the architect António Correia da Silva in a combination that was genuinely unusual for a market of its time: reinforced concrete alongside an iron structure, which gave him the large open spans the interior required while the exterior was dressed in the neoclassical granite language that Porto expected of its civic buildings. The result is a building that presents itself as one thing from the street and reveals itself as something rather different once you're inside.
You should be at one of the four street entrances — Rua Formosa to the south is the main one — so go in and stop at the edge of the ground floor to take in the full interior before you start moving through it.
Two levels, an open central courtyard, iron galleries running the full length of the upper floor on slender columns, corrugated metal and timber over the stalls below, and the whole thing flooded with the sounds and smells of a working food market that has been operating on this site, in various forms, for the better part of two centuries. The renovation completed in twenty twenty-two — the fourth attempt after three previous failures across three decades, this one finally successful after four years of work — introduced something worth looking down at as you walk: the aisles are named as streets, and the signs are set in traditional azulejo tiles of the kind used for street names across Porto, so the market's internal layout is mapped as though it were a neighbourhood, which is a small decision with a surprisingly large effect on how the place feels to move through.
[beat]
When the Portuguese government listed Bolhão as a Monument of Public Interest, the protection order covered two things: the Beaux-Arts building and its function as a traditional food market — not just the architecture, but the activity inside it, the vendors, the fish stalls, the flower sellers — which is not how heritage protection normally works, because most listed buildings are protected for what they look like rather than what they do. Around seventy traders work here daily, and the fish section is worth finding if you haven't already.

tours.longDescription:
Before this was a market, before it was even a square, it was waterlogged ground with a stream running through it — and where the stream pooled, it formed a bubble, which in Portuguese is bolhão, and that is how a food market in the centre of Porto came to be named after a marsh that no longer exists. The city council built on the site in 1839, but the building you see now came seventy-five years after that, in 1914, designed by António Correia da Silva in a combination genuinely unusual for a market of its time: reinforced concrete alongside an iron structure, which gave him the large open spans the interior required while the exterior was dressed in the neoclassical granite language Porto expected of its civic buildings. The result presents itself as one thing from the street and reveals itself as something rather different once you're inside — two levels, an open central courtyard, iron galleries running the full length of the upper floor on slender columns, corrugated metal and timber over the stalls below.

The renovation completed in 2022 — the fourth attempt after three previous failures across three decades, this one finally successful after four years of work — introduced something worth looking down at as you walk: the aisles are named as streets, and the signs are set in traditional azulejo tiles of the kind used for street names across Porto, so the market's internal layout is mapped as though it were a neighbourhood. When the Portuguese government listed Bolhão as a Monument of Public Interest, the protection order covered two things: the Beaux-Arts building and its function as a traditional food market — not just the architecture, but the activity inside it, the vendors, the fish stalls, the flower sellers — which is not how heritage protection normally works. Around seventy traders work here daily; the fish section is worth finding if you haven't already.
tours.shortDescription: Porto's Beaux-Arts food market — 1914 concrete-and-iron building protected for both its architecture AND its trading activity, recently renovated 2022.

tours.title: Serralves Museum of Contemporary Art

stops.title: Serralves Museum of Contemporary Art
stops.caption: Approach from Via Panorâmica on the western edge of the campus, then find the descending entry ramp.
stops.transcriptText:
The building you're about to enter was built where a vegetable garden used to be, which tells you something important about how seriously Siza took the existing site before he drew anything.
He spent years studying this location — the light, the slope of the land, the mature trees, and the pink Art Deco villa two hundred metres away that had been standing here since the nineteen thirties — and when he finally broke ground in nineteen eighty-five and finished in nineteen ninety-three, what he produced was a building whose most consistent quality is its reluctance to announce itself. You should be approaching from Via Panorâmica on the western edge of the campus, where the complex first becomes visible, and the first thing worth registering is that it doesn't read as a single building: ten separate volumes, each with its own character and proportion and relationship to the ground, arranged across the site in the way a small town might be rather than the way an institution usually is.
Find the entry ramp before you go in, because it's the first thing Siza designed here in the sense that matters.
The ramp descends from street level, narrows as you go down — the walls rise, the outside world drops away behind you — and then opens, without announcement, into a bright lobby, and that sequence of compression and release is not incidental plumbing between the car park and the museum but the first spatial sentence of a building that keeps doing exactly this. Take it slowly.
Inside, the U-shaped plan opens southward, with the two wings extending toward the garden and enclosing a courtyard between them where, in the centre, there is a magnolia tree that Siza described as the reason the U opens in that direction — he wanted the park to be able to enter the building, and the windows along the inner walls are positioned to frame specific trees and specific patches of sky rather than to provide general views, so the landscape outside is not a backdrop but a series of chosen moments that the building offers you one at a time. On the ramps connecting the gallery levels — Siza used ramps throughout, because a staircase breaks your gaze and forces you to watch your feet, while a ramp lets you keep looking at whatever is on the walls — the ceiling lighting distributes natural and artificial light evenly across the space through a system the museum's own materials describe as an inverted table, which produces a room without shadows from above and without the glare of spotlights.
[beat]
Before you leave, walk back outside and look toward the villa — pink, ornate, built for a textile magnate who filled it with René Lalique glass and Art Deco furniture — because Siza's museum, white and spare, sitting two hundred metres away in the same park, is in deliberate conversation with it, and the contrast between the two buildings, made sixty years apart on the same land, is the argument the whole estate is quietly making about what changes in architecture and what doesn't.

tours.longDescription:
The museum was built where a vegetable garden used to be, which tells you something important about how seriously Álvaro Siza took the existing site before he drew anything. He spent years studying this location — the light, the slope of the land, the mature trees, and the pink Art Deco villa two hundred metres away that had been standing here since the 1930s — and when he finally broke ground in 1985 and finished in 1999 (with parts continuing into the early 2000s), what he produced was a building whose most consistent quality is its reluctance to announce itself. From Via Panorâmica on the western edge of the campus, the complex first becomes visible: ten separate volumes, each with its own character and proportion and relationship to the ground, arranged across the site in the way a small town might be rather than the way an institution usually is.

The entry ramp is the first thing Siza designed here in the sense that matters. The ramp descends from street level, narrows as you go down — the walls rise, the outside world drops away behind you — and then opens, without announcement, into a bright lobby. That sequence of compression and release is not incidental plumbing between the car park and the museum but the first spatial sentence of a building that keeps doing exactly this. Inside, the U-shaped plan opens southward, with the two wings extending toward the garden and enclosing a courtyard between them where, in the centre, there is a magnolia tree that Siza described as the reason the U opens in that direction — he wanted the park to be able to enter the building, and the windows along the inner walls are positioned to frame specific trees and specific patches of sky rather than to provide general views. On the ramps connecting the gallery levels (Siza used ramps throughout, because a staircase breaks your gaze and forces you to watch your feet, while a ramp lets you keep looking at whatever is on the walls) the ceiling lighting distributes natural and artificial light evenly through what the museum's own materials describe as an inverted table, producing a room without shadows from above and without the glare of spotlights. Outside, the pink villa — built for a textile magnate who filled it with René Lalique glass and Art Deco furniture — sits two hundred metres away in the same park, and the contrast between the two buildings, made sixty years apart on the same land, is the argument the whole estate is quietly making about what changes in architecture and what doesn't.
tours.shortDescription: Siza's white modernist museum in Porto — ten volumes arranged like a small town, in deliberate conversation with the pink Art Deco villa across the park.

tours.title: Beacon Theatre

stops.title: Beacon Theatre
stops.caption: Stand on Broadway between 74th and 75th Streets, under the blade marquee.
stops.transcriptText:
For twenty-five years, the Allman Brothers played a March residency on this stage.

On Broadway between 74th and 75th Streets, the Beacon Theatre rises above the street with its blade marquee and three-story ornamental facade — a 1929 movie palace that has been continuously operating, in one form or another, for nearly a hundred years. The architect was Walter Ahlschlager, and the theatre opened in December 1929, just weeks after the stock market crash, as a combined vaudeville and movie venue with two thousand eight hundred and ninety-four seats — at the time, the largest theatre on the Upper West Side.

The interior is the part that matters. Ahlschlager designed it as a fantasy of accumulated grandeur: gold leaf, painted murals, plaster sculpture, a vast vaulted ceiling decorated with classical figures, columns ornamented with Roman, Greek, Renaissance, and Rococo elements all at once. It is what was called, at the time, an "atmospheric theatre" — a building designed to make moviegoers feel they were entering somewhere worth being.

Most theatres of this type were demolished in the second half of the twentieth century. Movie palaces of the 1920s were enormous buildings with enormous heating bills, and as suburban multiplexes took over American moviegoing, the Beacons of New York were torn down one by one. The Beacon survived in part by accident — it became a rock concert venue in the 1970s, then a Madison Square Garden Entertainment property in the 2000s, and was restored in 2008 in a sixteen-million-dollar renovation.

[beat]

The Allman Brothers Band first played the Beacon in 1989, then returned in 1992, and made it an annual tradition: every March, for fifteen to twenty consecutive nights, the band would play at the Beacon and nowhere else in New York. The residency ran every year until 2014, when guitarist Derek Trucks left the band — and effectively ended the band's New York presence. Over those twenty-five years, the band played more than two hundred nights at the Beacon, and most of the band's bootlegs and live recordings from that era are taken from this stage.

The marquee still announces shows nearly every night of the year. Walk close to the entrance to see the ticket booth, the lobby behind the doors, and the original box office windows — the Beacon is one of the only major concert venues in New York where the building itself is worth seeing, even if you're not seeing a show.

tours.longDescription:
On Broadway between 74th and 75th Streets, the Beacon Theatre rises above the street with its blade marquee and three-story ornamental facade — a 1929 movie palace that has been continuously operating, in one form or another, for nearly a hundred years. The architect was Walter Ahlschlager, and the theatre opened in December 1929, just weeks after the stock market crash, as a combined vaudeville and movie venue with 2,894 seats — at the time, the largest theatre on the Upper West Side. The interior is the part that matters: Ahlschlager designed it as a fantasy of accumulated grandeur — gold leaf, painted murals, plaster sculpture, a vast vaulted ceiling decorated with classical figures, columns ornamented with Roman, Greek, Renaissance, and Rococo elements all at once. It is what was called, at the time, an "atmospheric theatre" — a building designed to make moviegoers feel they were entering somewhere worth being.

Most theatres of this type were demolished in the second half of the twentieth century. Movie palaces of the 1920s were enormous buildings with enormous heating bills, and as suburban multiplexes took over American moviegoing, the Beacons of New York were torn down one by one. The Beacon survived in part by accident — it became a rock concert venue in the 1970s, then a Madison Square Garden Entertainment property in the 2000s, and was restored in 2008 in a $16M renovation. The Allman Brothers Band first played the Beacon in 1989, then returned in 1992, and made it an annual tradition: every March, for fifteen to twenty consecutive nights, the band would play at the Beacon and nowhere else in New York. The residency ran every year until 2014, when guitarist Derek Trucks left the band. Over those twenty-five years, the band played more than two hundred nights here, and most of the band's bootlegs and live recordings from that era are taken from this stage.
tours.shortDescription: The 1929 Upper West Side movie palace turned premier concert hall — home of the Allman Brothers' twenty-five-year March residency.

tours.title: Chelsea Market

stops.title: Chelsea Market
stops.caption: Enter the concourse on Ninth Avenue between 15th and 16th Streets.
stops.transcriptText:
The Oreo cookie was invented inside this building.

Chelsea Market occupies the entire block between Fifteenth and Sixteenth Streets on Ninth Avenue. The exterior is dark red brick, with industrial windows and the original factory loading bays still visible. The High Line, the elevated park you may have walked, runs directly through the upper floors of the building — entering on the south side, exiting on the north.

The factory was built in 1898 as the headquarters and main production facility of the National Biscuit Company, later known as Nabisco. For most of the early twentieth century, this building was one of the largest food manufacturing operations in New York. The Saltine cracker was made here. So was the Fig Newton, the Mallomar, and most notably, the Oreo — which was invented in 1912 by Nabisco chemists working in this building, on a then-experimental cream filling encased between two chocolate wafers. The recipe has been essentially unchanged for over a hundred years.

The factory closed in the 1950s. The building sat partially empty for decades. In the 1990s, the developer Irwin Cohen bought it and began converting it into mixed-use space — preserving the industrial character of the building while adding a long ground-floor concourse of food vendors. Chelsea Market opened in 1997. The upper floors became offices, eventually occupied by Google, which bought the entire upper portion of the building in 2018 for nearly two and a half billion dollars.

[beat]

What survives is the architecture, and the food. The concourse runs the length of the block, with about thirty-five vendors — bakeries, oyster bars, lobster rolls, tacos, Japanese knife shops, the original Amy's Bread, the Lobster Place, Los Tacos No. 1. The building is also where the Food Network operated for many years; their old studio is still on the south side. About six million people visit each year, making it one of the most popular food halls in the world.

Inside, the concourse runs the length of the building. It takes about ten minutes if you don't stop. Most people stop.

tours.shortDescription: The Meatpacking District food hall inside the 1898 Nabisco factory where the Oreo was invented — thirty-five vendors, six million visitors a year, the High Line running overhead.

tours.title: Radio City Music Hall

stops.title: Radio City Music Hall
stops.caption: Stand on Sixth Avenue at 50th Street and look up at the vertical Art Deco marquee.
stops.transcriptText:
When this theater opened in 1932, it was the largest indoor theater in the world.

Look up. The marquee runs nearly the entire facade — the most famous Art Deco marquee in New York, with the words "RADIO CITY MUSIC HALL" in massive vertical letters. The building is on Sixth Avenue at 50th Street, part of the Rockefeller Center complex, designed and built at the same time as the rest of it, opening on December 27, 1932.

The architect was Edward Durell Stone — the same architect who designed the original Lollipop Building at Columbus Circle. The interior was designed by Donald Deskey, one of the major American Art Deco designers, and is considered one of the most fully realized Art Deco interiors in the world. The auditorium itself is the gold standard for what an Art Deco theater could be: a fan-shaped ceiling that rises in concentric curves, walls in muted gold and sunset colors, six thousand seats arranged in three deep balconies.

The theater was originally intended as a vaudeville house. The opening-night program was a six-hour extravaganza of dance numbers, comedy sketches, animal acts, and live music. The audience hated it. Within weeks, the management changed format — moving to a combination of first-run movies and live stage shows, a model that Radio City pioneered.

The other thing that started early at Radio City was the Rockettes. The precision dance group — formed in 1925 in St. Louis, originally called the Missouri Rockets — moved to Radio City in 1932 as a permanent feature of the stage shows. The Christmas Spectacular, an annual holiday production built around the Rockettes, has run every December at Radio City since 1933. It is one of the longest-running theatrical productions in American history.

[beat]

Today the theater runs about three hundred events a year — concerts, award shows, the Tony Awards, and the Christmas Spectacular every winter. The interior is largely unchanged from 1932. If you have a chance to see anything inside, the building is the reason to go.

Walk under the marquee. The Deskey-designed lobby is visible through the doors. Even the parts of Radio City visible from the sidewalk are some of the most carefully designed public surfaces in New York.

tours.longDescription:
Look up. The marquee runs nearly the entire facade — the most famous Art Deco marquee in New York, with the words RADIO CITY MUSIC HALL in massive vertical letters. The building is on Sixth Avenue at 50th Street, part of the Rockefeller Center complex, designed and built at the same time as the rest of it, opening on December 27, 1932. The architect was Edward Durell Stone; the interior was designed by Donald Deskey, one of the major American Art Deco designers, and is considered one of the most fully realized Art Deco interiors in the world. The auditorium itself is the gold standard for what an Art Deco theater could be: a fan-shaped ceiling that rises in concentric curves, walls in muted gold and sunset colors, six thousand seats arranged in three deep balconies.

The theater was originally intended as a vaudeville house. Within weeks the management changed format, moving to a combination of first-run movies and live stage shows, a model Radio City pioneered. tours.shortDescription: The 1932 Art Deco landmark at Rockefeller Center — six thousand seats, the Rockettes since opening night, the Christmas Spectacular every December for ninety years.

tours.title: Tenement Museum

stops.title: Tenement Museum
stops.caption: Stand outside 97 Orchard Street on the Lower East Side; the visitor center is at 103.
stops.transcriptText:
This building was sealed shut for fifty years, which is why it survived.

In front of you, at 97 Orchard Street, is the Tenement Museum — a five-story brick building, narrow and tall, that looks like every other Lower East Side tenement from the same era because that's exactly what it is. The building was constructed in 1863 by a German immigrant named Lukas Glockner as rental housing for the wave of immigrants arriving on the Lower East Side in the post-Civil War decades. Between 1863 and 1935, approximately seven thousand people from twenty different countries lived inside.

What makes the building unique is what happened next. In 1935, after seventy-two years of continuous occupation, the upper floors were closed when new tenement laws required improvements the landlord didn't want to make. The ground-floor shops kept operating, but the apartments above them were locked, abandoned, and forgotten. For fifty-three years, nobody lived in 97 Orchard Street, and the building became, in effect, a time capsule — the wallpaper from the 1880s stayed on the walls, the linoleum floors from the 1910s stayed on the floors, and the layouts, fixtures, and painted moldings were simply left in place.

In 1988, a historian named Ruth Abram was looking for a building to use as an immigration museum, and she found this one. The upper floors had not been seen by anyone other than the landlord since the 1930s, and Abram and her co-founder Anita Jacobson realized that what they had was not a place to build a museum but a museum that had already built itself. They restored individual apartments to reflect the lives of actual identifiable former residents — German garment workers, Italian Catholic seamstresses, Jewish refugees, Greek shopkeepers — based on census records, court documents, and oral histories from descendants.

[beat]

What you can do here that you can't do anywhere else in New York is walk into the actual apartment of a specific identified family from a specific year, and see what their kitchen looked like, what their walls were like, and where their children slept. The tours are small, eight to ten people at a time, and they take place inside the recreated apartments themselves. The visitor center is at 103 Orchard, just south of here, and contains more recent apartments from later immigrant waves — Puerto Rican, Chinese, Bangladeshi families.

The exterior is plain — the historical weight is inside.

tours.title: The Woolworth Building

stops.title: The Woolworth Building
stops.caption: Stand on Broadway across from City Hall Park looking up at 233 Broadway.
stops.transcriptText:
Frank Woolworth paid for this building in cash.

Look up. The Gothic Revival skyscraper in front of you, on Broadway across from City Hall Park, is the Woolworth Building — completed in 1913, 792 feet tall, sixty stories, the tallest building in the world for seventeen years. The exterior is white terra cotta and limestone, carved with Gothic detailing: pointed arches, flying buttresses, gargoyles, a steep central tower. The clergyman who delivered the opening speech in 1913 called it the "Cathedral of Commerce," and the name stuck.

Frank Winfield Woolworth made his money one nickel at a time. He had opened a "five-cent store" in Utica, New York, in 1879, selling small household goods at a fixed low price — needles, soap, pots, candy — but the store failed. He tried again in Lancaster, Pennsylvania, with a five-and-ten-cent format, and that one worked. By the early 1900s, F.W. Woolworth had built a chain of nearly six hundred stores across the United States and Britain, and he had become one of the richest men in America.

He commissioned the building in 1910 from the architect Cass Gilbert, who would later design the U.S. Supreme Court building in Washington. Gilbert gave him Gothic Revival — a style associated with cathedrals, universities, and old money — which was exactly the signal Woolworth wanted to send. The building cost thirteen and a half million dollars, and Woolworth paid in cash, in full, with no mortgage. The lobby is decorated with mosaics, marble, and bronze, and a bas-relief above one elevator shows Woolworth himself, counting coins.

[beat]

What's worth knowing, looking up at this building, is what it represented at the moment it opened. In 1913, the skyline of New York City was about to change permanently. The Woolworth Building was the first true skyscraper to be built primarily as a corporate symbol — the headquarters of a single company, designed to advertise that company's wealth and reach. The pattern it established would define the next century of American corporate architecture: every major company that has built a flagship skyscraper since — Chrysler, Empire State, Seagram, Citigroup, One World Trade — is, in some sense, building a more modern version of the same idea.

The upper floors are now luxury condominiums, and the lobby is occasionally open for tours, by appointment. Walk in if you can — the marble and the mosaic ceiling are genuinely unlike anything else in New York.

tours.title: The Dakota

stops.title: The Dakota
stops.caption: Stand at the corner of Central Park West and 72nd Street, across from the building's entry archway.
stops.transcriptText:
The Dakota was named the Dakota because it was so far from downtown Manhattan.

At the corner of Central Park West and 72nd Street is the Dakota — one of the most famous apartment buildings in the United States, and one of the first true luxury apartment buildings ever built in New York. The exterior is German Renaissance: dark sandstone, gabled roofs, dormers, balconies, the kind of building that looks like it was lifted out of central Europe and dropped onto Central Park West. It was designed by Henry Janeway Hardenbergh, who later designed the Plaza Hotel.

Construction began in 1880, commissioned by Edward Cabot Clark, who had made his fortune as a partner in the Singer Sewing Machine company. The neighborhood, at the time, was almost entirely undeveloped — the West 70s were farmland, the streets unpaved, the subway not yet imagined — and Clark's friends thought he was insane to build a luxury apartment building in the middle of nowhere. The name, which Clark chose himself, was a joke at his own expense: the Dakota Territory, in the 1880s, was the most remote part of the United States, and he was building, the joke went, in a Dakota of his own.

The building opened in 1884 with 65 apartments, ranging from four to twenty rooms each. The lobby is gas-lit, and the central courtyard is one of the few interior open spaces of its kind in New York. Over the next century, the building became the home of a remarkable concentration of cultural figures — Leonard Bernstein, Lauren Bacall, Rudolf Nureyev, Judy Holliday, the writer Saul Bellow, and most famously, John Lennon and Yoko Ono.

On December 8, 1980, Lennon was shot in the entry archway of the Dakota and died at Roosevelt Hospital later that night. Yoko Ono still lives in the building.

[beat]

What's worth knowing, looking at the Dakota, is that it set a template that defined the rest of New York's history. Before the Dakota, "apartment" was a word associated with tenements — crowded, low-status, working-class housing. The Dakota proved that wealthy New Yorkers would live in apartments if the apartments were grand enough, and within twenty years the West Side had filled in, with Central Park West lined by buildings imitating the Dakota's model.

Across the street, in Central Park, is a small memorial called Strawberry Fields, about a hundred yards inside the park gate.

tours.title: Lever House

stops.title: Lever House
stops.caption: Stand on Park Avenue at 53rd Street, looking up at the glass tower over its open plaza.
stops.transcriptText:
Before this building, no skyscraper in New York had walls of glass.

On Park Avenue at 53rd Street stands Lever House — built in 1952, the first true glass curtain wall skyscraper in New York City. The building is twenty-one stories of green-tinted glass and stainless steel, sitting on a low horizontal base that floats above an open plaza at street level. The architect was Gordon Bunshaft of Skidmore, Owings and Merrill — the firm known as SOM, which would go on to design Sears Tower, the original World Trade Center, Burj Khalifa, and most of the major American corporate skyscrapers of the second half of the twentieth century. Lever House was Bunshaft's first major commission and the project that established the model.

The building was commissioned by Lever Brothers — the British soap and detergent company best known in America for Dove, Lifebuoy, and Pepsodent. Lever Brothers wanted a New York headquarters that would feel modern, forward-looking, and clean — qualities they associated with their products — so they asked Bunshaft for a building that would look unlike anything else in the city, and he gave them a tower of glass.

Lever House broke with every convention of New York skyscraper design. Earlier skyscrapers — the Woolworth, the Chrysler, the Empire State — were stone-clad buildings that filled their entire lots and rose in setbacks dictated by 1916 zoning law, whereas Lever House occupied only a fraction of the lot, set back from the street to leave a public plaza at ground level. The walls were glass instead of stone, and the steel structure was expressed honestly through the facade rather than hidden behind ornament. It was, essentially, a European modernist building dropped onto Park Avenue.

[beat]

The impact was immediate. Within a decade, every major corporate headquarters in New York was being built in the same style — Mies van der Rohe's Seagram Building, across the street, completed in 1958, took the same approach further. The CBS Building, the Pan Am Building, Citigroup Center, the World Trade Center — every one of them traces its lineage to Lever House. The city's 1961 zoning law was rewritten to encourage exactly the plaza-set-back model that Lever House introduced, and New York's skyline, from the 1960s onward, is essentially the answer to a question this building asked.

The plaza at the base is open to the public — walk through it. The ground-floor gallery space, on the south side, often has free contemporary art exhibitions.

tours.shortDescription: Gordon Bunshaft's 1952 glass-and-steel tower on Park Avenue — New York's first true curtain-wall skyscraper and the model every postwar corporate headquarters in the city is copying.

tours.title: Seagram Building

stops.title: Seagram Building
stops.caption: Stand on the Seagram Plaza on Park Avenue between 52nd and 53rd Streets, near one of the reflecting pools.
stops.transcriptText:
The plaza you're standing on changed American zoning law.

You're on the Seagram Plaza, on Park Avenue between 52nd and 53rd Streets. The building rising in front of you — thirty-eight stories of bronze-and-amber glass, set back fifty feet from the street — is the Seagram Building, designed by the German architect Mies van der Rohe with Philip Johnson, completed in 1958. It is one of the most influential buildings in the history of architecture, and almost every other skyscraper built in the world since 1960 is, in some sense, a version of this one.

The building exists because of a twenty-seven-year-old woman named Phyllis Lambert. In 1954, Lambert was living in Paris when she received word that her father, Samuel Bronfman — the head of the Seagram Distilling Company — had commissioned a New York headquarters that he was unhappy with. Phyllis wrote him a long letter explaining why the design was bad, then flew home to take charge of the project herself. She traveled to architecture schools across the United States, interviewed leading architects, and concluded that the building should be designed by Mies van der Rohe, then teaching in Chicago. She convinced her father, and the Seagram Building is the result.

Mies designed almost everything you can see. The building's bronze curtain wall — the first bronze curtain wall ever built — has the I-beams of the building's structure expressed on the exterior, even though they're not structural; they're symbolic, indicating the building's frame to the viewer. The plaza in front, with its two reflecting pools, was a radical decision that cost Seagram an entire usable floor of office space — in exchange, the building got something almost no Park Avenue tower had: room to breathe.

[beat]

The plaza is what changed New York. When the city revised its zoning code in 1961, it borrowed directly from what Seagram had done — offering developers extra floor area in exchange for providing public open space at street level — and within ten years, every new tower on Park Avenue and Sixth Avenue had a Seagram-style plaza out front. Most of them are worse — smaller, windier, more hostile to the public, with fewer benches and meaner proportions — but the model is the same, and New York's modern skyline is, essentially, a series of failed attempts to copy this building.

If you have a few minutes, sit on one of the granite walls bordering the pools. The plaza was specifically designed to be comfortable to sit in — most of its imitators were not.

tours.longDescription:

The building exists because of a twenty-seven-year-old woman named Phyllis Lambert. The plaza in front, with its two reflecting pools, was a radical decision that cost Seagram an entire usable floor of office space — in exchange, the building got something almost no Park Avenue tower had: room to breathe. The plaza is what changed New York. When the city revised its zoning code in 1961, it borrowed directly from what Seagram had done — offering developers extra floor area in exchange for providing public open space at street level — and within ten years every new tower on Park Avenue and Sixth Avenue had a Seagram-style plaza out front. Most of them are worse — smaller, windier, more hostile to the public, with fewer benches and meaner proportions — but the model is the same, and New York's modern skyline is essentially a series of failed attempts to copy this building.
tours.shortDescription: Mies van der Rohe and Philip Johnson's 1958 bronze-and-glass tower on Park Avenue — the canonical International Style skyscraper and the plaza that rewrote New York's 1961 zoning code.

tours.title: Stonewall National Monument

stops.title: Stonewall National Monument
stops.caption: Stand inside Christopher Park, near the white George Segal figures, looking across Christopher Street toward the Stonewall Inn.
stops.transcriptText:
The modern gay rights movement started across the street.

Christopher Park is the small triangular park you're standing in, tucked between Christopher Street, Grove Street, and West Fourth Street in Greenwich Village. The brick building you can see across Christopher Street, with the red brick facade and the small marquee reading "STONEWALL," is the Stonewall Inn — a bar that, in 1969, was one of the only places in New York where gay and lesbian and transgender people could gather openly. On June 28th of that year, at around one-thirty in the morning, the New York Police Department raided the bar. Raids of gay bars were routine in the 1960s, but this one went differently — the patrons fought back.

The first night's confrontation lasted several hours, with crowds gathering outside the bar and around this park, and the police barricaded inside the Stonewall Inn awaiting reinforcements. The next night the crowds came back, and the night after that, and the night after that — six consecutive nights of protests, with police charges, broken windows, and improvised marches through the surrounding streets. By the end of the week, the gay community of Lower Manhattan had publicly organized itself in a way it had never done before. The first Pride march, exactly one year later in June 1970, traced a route from this park north to Central Park, and it has happened every June since.

The white painted figures in the park around you are part of a sculpture by the artist George Segal called "Gay Liberation," installed in 1992: two standing men and two seated women, painted entirely white, in postures of casual intimacy. It was one of the first public sculptures in the United States to depict gay and lesbian relationships.

[beat]

In June of 2016, President Obama designated Christopher Park, the Stonewall Inn, and the surrounding streets the Stonewall National Monument — the first national monument in the United States dedicated to LGBTQ history. A small visitor center opened on the corner of the park in 2024, and the Stonewall Inn itself is still operating as a working bar, more than fifty years after the night that made it famous.

The park is small enough to walk through in a minute — the history is what makes it worth standing here for longer.

tours.longDescription:
Christopher Park is the small triangular park tucked between Christopher Street, Grove Street, and West Fourth Street in Greenwich Village — and it is the heart of the Stonewall National Monument, the broader federal site that President Obama designated in June 2016 as the first national monument in the United States dedicated to LGBTQ history. The monument takes in Christopher Park itself, the Stonewall Inn across the street, and the surrounding blocks where six consecutive nights of protest in June 1969 began the modern gay rights movement. The brick building across Christopher Street, with the red facade and the small marquee reading "STONEWALL," is the Stonewall Inn — in 1969 one of the only places in New York where gay, lesbian, and transgender people could gather openly. On the night of June 28th the NYPD raided it; the patrons fought back; the crowd in this park grew larger every night for a week. The first Pride march, one year later, traced a route from this park north to Central Park.

This tour stands inside the park itself and treats the federal monument as the subject — distinct from the Stonewall Inn tour, which stands outside the bar and focuses on the 1969 uprising. The white painted figures around you are part of George Segal's 1992 sculpture "Gay Liberation," installed by the city of New York: two standing men and two seated women in postures of casual intimacy, one of the first public sculptures in the United States to depict gay and lesbian relationships. A small National Park Service visitor center opened at the corner of the park in 2024, the first NPS visitor center in the country devoted to LGBTQ history. The Stonewall Inn is still operating as a working bar, more than fifty years after the night that made it famous.
tours.shortDescription: The 2016 federal monument inside Christopher Park — the first US national monument dedicated to LGBTQ history, taking in Christopher Park, the Stonewall Inn, George Segal's Gay Liberation sculpture, and the 2024 NPS visitor center.

tours.title: TWA Hotel at JFK

stops.title: TWA Hotel at JFK
stops.caption: Stand outside JFK Terminal 5, looking at the curving concrete shells of the original TWA Flight Center head house.
stops.transcriptText:
Eero Saarinen designed this building to look like a bird about to fly. He died before it opened.

At JFK Airport, just outside terminal five, sits the TWA Flight Center — designed by the Finnish-American architect Eero Saarinen and completed in 1962. The form is unmistakable: two great concrete shells curving up and outward like wings about to lift off, supported by minimal columns, an entire facade of glass between them. From across the parking lot, the building looks like it's pulling itself up off the ground, and from inside, every surface, every staircase, every floor edge is rounded.

The building was commissioned in 1955 by Trans World Airlines — TWA — at the height of the Jet Age, the moment when air travel was being transformed from a luxury into a mass-market industry. Saarinen was given the freedom to design something that would express the optimism of the moment, and he worked on it for six years. He died of a brain tumor in September of 1961, eleven months before the building opened, at the age of fifty-one.

The terminal operated for thirty-nine years. TWA itself went into a long decline through the 1980s and 90s, hit by deregulation, by a series of crashes, and by an aging fleet. The airline declared bankruptcy in 2001, and the terminal was closed in October of that year. For the next seventeen years, it sat empty — too significant to demolish, too inefficient for modern air travel to reuse as a working terminal.

In 2019, the building reopened as the TWA Hotel — the original Saarinen head house preserved as a lobby, with two new hotel towers built behind it. The interior — the curved staircases, the original signage, the round red conversation pit, the long sweeping tubes that connected the head house to the gates — has been restored to its 1962 appearance, almost obsessively.

[beat]

What's striking about the place is that the building is what survived. The airline is gone, the Jet Age is gone, the model of travel the building was made for — leisurely, formal, optimistic — is gone. The architecture, which was supposed to be the most ephemeral part of the whole enterprise, is the part that's still here. You can sit at the bar in the round red conversation pit and have a martini, in the same room Don Draper would have ordered one.

The hotel is open to the public — walk through, sit in the lobby. The pool, on the tarmac, is for guests only.

tours.shortDescription: Eero Saarinen's 1962 winged-concrete head house at JFK Terminal 5 — closed for seventeen years after TWA's bankruptcy, restored almost obsessively as a hotel lobby in 2019.

tours.title: Bethesda Terrace

stops.title: Bethesda Terrace
stops.caption: Stand at the top of Bethesda Terrace in Central Park, looking down the great stone staircase toward the Angel of the Waters fountain on the lower terrace.
stops.transcriptText:
The angel in front of you is the first major public sculpture by a woman in New York.

You're at the top of the Bethesda Terrace, the central architectural feature of Central Park. The two stone staircases descend on either side to the lower terrace and the Bethesda Fountain at its center, beyond which the lake opens out toward the rowboats and the trees of the Ramble. The terrace was designed by Calvert Vaux and Jacob Wrey Mould as the only formal architectural composition in the entire eight hundred and forty-three acres of the park — Frederick Law Olmsted's design treated nearly the whole park as a managed natural landscape, and Bethesda was the one place where stone, geometry, and ceremony were allowed to take over.

The fountain at the center of the lower terrace was completed in 1873 and is called "Angel of the Waters." It was sculpted by Emma Stebbins, an American expatriate working in Rome, and it was the first major public sculpture commission awarded to a woman by the city of New York. The angel atop the fountain holds a lily in one hand and gestures downward with the other, blessing the water — a reference to the Pool of Bethesda in the Gospel of John, where an angel is said to have stirred the waters with healing properties.

If you walk to the lower terrace and look up under the great staircase, you'll see the most surprising feature of the entire composition: a vaulted tile ceiling, covered in approximately fifteen thousand individually patterned encaustic tiles made in England by the Minton company in the 1860s. It is the only Minton tile installation of this scale anywhere in the United States, and it was nearly lost — the tiles were removed for safekeeping in 1983 and were not reinstalled until 2007, after a multi-year restoration.

[beat]

The terrace is the part of Central Park most photographed in films and television — the fountain has appeared in everything from "Hair" to "Home Alone 2" to "When Harry Met Sally" — but more than that, it is the part of the park where the public actually gathers. Buskers play here, couples are photographed here, and people meet here, the way they do in great urban plazas across the world.

The terrace sits roughly in the geographic center of the park, and the Mall, with its statues and its allée of American elms, leads directly here from the south. It is the most direct way to walk down to the fountain from the southern entrances.

tours.shortDescription: Calvert Vaux and Jacob Wrey Mould's 1864 stone terrace at the heart of Central Park — the only formal architectural composition in Olmsted's managed landscape, anchored by Emma Stebbins's *Angel of the Waters*, the city's first major public sculpture commission awarded to a woman.

tours.title: Cathedral of St. John the Divine

stops.title: Cathedral of St. John the Divine
stops.caption: Stand on Amsterdam Avenue at 112th Street, facing the western front of the Cathedral of Saint John the Divine.
stops.transcriptText:
This cathedral has been under construction for one hundred and thirty-four years.

You're on Amsterdam Avenue at 112th Street, outside the Cathedral of St. John the Divine — the seat of the Episcopal Diocese of New York and the largest Anglican cathedral in the world. Construction began in 1892, with a Romanesque-Byzantine design by the architects Heins and LaFarge. In 1911, after the early structure of the choir had been completed, the cathedral's leadership changed direction and brought in a new architect, Ralph Adams Cram, who redesigned the rest of the building in French Gothic. The result is that the eastern half is Romanesque, the western half is Gothic, and the building, more than a hundred years later, is still not complete.

The exterior is six hundred and one feet long and two hundred and thirty-two feet wide, with two towers planned for the western front that have never been built above the height of the lower roofline. The interior is even more impressive: the central nave is wider than that of any Gothic cathedral in Europe except Cologne, the rose window on the western front is forty feet across, and the floor area is approximately one hundred and twenty-four thousand square feet — depending on how it's measured, either the largest cathedral in the world or one of the two largest.

The cathedral has acquired a nickname, "St. John the Unfinished," and there is something accurate and not entirely affectionate in the phrase. Construction has paused multiple times: during the First World War, during the Depression, during the Second World War, and after a major fire in 2001 that closed the building for several years. Each time, the work has resumed slowly, with funds raised piecemeal, and each time, the project has gotten a little further.

[beat]

What is unusual about St. John's, beyond its size, is what it has become. The cathedral has, over the past century, evolved into one of the most architecturally and culturally inclusive religious spaces in the world — interfaith services are held inside, the Dalai Lama has preached here, an AIDS memorial is housed here, and the annual Blessing of the Animals draws hundreds of New Yorkers and their pets to the nave on the feast of Saint Francis. The cathedral functions, in practice, as a kind of civic temple as much as a religious one.

The exterior gardens are open to the public, and the interior is open every day. The walk through the nave, from the front doors to the high altar, takes a noticeable amount of time — the building is genuinely too big to take in from outside.

tours.title: The Astor Place Cube

stops.title: The Astor Place Cube
stops.caption: Stand at the traffic island where Cooper Square, Lafayette Street, and Fourth Avenue converge, beside the corner-balanced black steel cube.
stops.transcriptText:
If you push hard enough, this sculpture spins.

You're at Astor Place, where Cooper Square, Lafayette Street, and Fourth Avenue converge in the East Village. The black steel cube in the middle of the traffic island, eight feet on each side, mounted on a single point at one corner, is called "The Alamo." It is a sculpture by the American artist Tony Rosenthal, installed in 1967 as part of a New York City public art program. The cube weighs about a ton, sits on a vertical pole that runs through one diagonal axis of the cube, and rotates on that axis when pushed by several people working together. The spinning, depending on who is pushing, can range from a slow shuffle to a surprisingly fast revolution.

Rosenthal designed and fabricated the cube specifically for this site, and it was supposed to be a temporary installation — one of a number of works that were placed around the city in 1967 to test how public sculpture would be received by ordinary New Yorkers. The reception of "The Alamo" was, by every measurement, extraordinary. Within days of its installation, people had figured out that it would spin, and groups of students from nearby Cooper Union, NYU, and the public schools were gathering at the cube to push it around in their lunch breaks. The temporary installation became permanent in 1968.

The cube has been continuously at Astor Place ever since, with one major interruption: in 2014, it was removed for restoration and sent to a foundry in Philadelphia for several months of work, during which the corners were welded, the surface was repainted, and the rotation mechanism was repaired. When it was returned, the city held a small ceremony, and someone — predictably — pushed it.

[beat]

What makes the Alamo specifically distinctive, beyond the fact that it spins, is its role in the cultural geography of the East Village. The cube is the unofficial meeting point of the neighborhood — punks gathered here in the 1980s, skateboarders gather here now, students still meet here between classes, and the cube has appeared in films, photographs, and graffiti as a kind of shorthand for downtown New York. It is one of the very few public sculptures in the city that the public actually uses, rather than simply photographs.

If you want to push it, you'll usually need at least three or four other people — it's heavier than it looks. The Cooper Union building across the plaza, with its sharp angular metal facade, is the work of architect Thom Mayne, and is worth a look in its own right.

tours.title: The Plaza Hotel

stops.title: The Plaza Hotel
stops.caption: Stand at Grand Army Plaza at 5th Avenue and 59th Street, looking up at the white-glazed-brick French Renaissance facade of the Plaza Hotel.
stops.transcriptText:
The architect who designed the Dakota also designed this hotel, twenty-three years later.

You're at the corner of 5th Avenue and 59th Street, at Grand Army Plaza, looking up at the Plaza Hotel — a nineteen-story French Renaissance chateau in white glazed brick, with a mansard roof, ornate dormers, gold trim, and the words "THE PLAZA" lettered across the front. The architect was Henry Janeway Hardenbergh, who had previously designed the Dakota apartment building on Central Park West, twenty-three years earlier in 1884. By the time the Plaza opened in 1907, Hardenbergh had perfected the formula: a luxurious European-style residential building that brought the architectural vocabulary of Paris and Munich to American streets, with American interior amenities and American scale.

The Plaza was, from the beginning, designed for both permanent residents and short-stay guests. It had eight hundred rooms, four hundred bathrooms, two of the largest ballrooms in New York, and four restaurants. The clientele over the first century included Mark Twain, F. Scott Fitzgerald, Ernest Hemingway, the Beatles in 1964 — they stayed during their first American tour, surrounded by fans on every side of the building — and Truman Capote, whose 1966 Black and White Ball in the Grand Ballroom is still considered the most famous private party in twentieth-century New York. The hotel was also the fictional home of Eloise, the precocious six-year-old in Kay Thompson's 1955 children's book, and her portrait still hangs in the lobby.

In 2008, two-thirds of the rooms were converted into private condominiums — a financial decision driven by the Plaza's then-owner, the Israeli developer Elad Properties, and a controversial one. The Plaza had been a working hotel for a hundred and one years, and the conversion reduced the number of hotel rooms to around two hundred and eighty. The lobby remains open to the public, however, and the Palm Court, the iconic restaurant under the stained-glass ceiling, still serves breakfast, tea, and a steady diet of tourist photographs.

[beat]

What's worth knowing, standing at Grand Army Plaza, is that the Plaza is the only one of New York's great Gilded Age grand hotels still operating from its original 1907 building, in something resembling its original use. The Astor, the Knickerbocker, the Waldorf-Astoria, the Vanderbilt — all of them either demolished, converted to offices, or rebuilt in different locations. The Plaza is the survivor.

Walk into the lobby if you want — it is open to the public, the Palm Court is on the ground floor to the right, and the elaborate gold ceilings are unchanged from when Hardenbergh finished them.

tours.title: Trinity Church

stops.title: Trinity Church
stops.caption: Stand at the head of Wall Street where it meets Broadway, facing the brownstone Gothic Revival facade and copper spire of Trinity Church.
stops.transcriptText:
Alexander Hamilton is buried in the cemetery in front of you.

You're at the head of Wall Street, where it meets Broadway, looking at Trinity Church — a Gothic Revival church in brownstone, with a tall central tower topped by a copper steeple. The current building was completed in 1846 and is the third church to stand on this site, which has been the home of Trinity Parish since 1697, when King William the Third of England granted the land to the Anglican congregation. The architect was Richard Upjohn, who would later design the Gothic Revival main gate of Green-Wood Cemetery in Brooklyn, and the church was, when completed, the tallest building in New York City. It held that distinction for twenty-three years.

The graveyard in front of you, between the church and Broadway, has been in use since the late seventeenth century, and is the burial place of a number of figures from the founding generation of the United States. Alexander Hamilton, the first Secretary of the Treasury, was buried here after his death in the duel with Aaron Burr in 1804, and his tomb is the larger square monument near the southern edge of the cemetery. His wife Eliza Schuyler Hamilton, who outlived him by fifty years, is buried next to him. Robert Fulton, who developed the first commercially successful steamboat, is also buried here, along with the second Secretary of the Treasury Albert Gallatin, and several signers of the Declaration of Independence.

The graveyard is unusual in lower Manhattan in several ways: most colonial-era cemeteries in the area were relocated or built over in the nineteenth century, and Trinity's survival is partly a function of the church's continuous ownership and its formal designation as an active religious institution rather than a public burial ground.

[beat]

What's worth knowing about Trinity, standing here, is that it survived the events of September 11, 2001. The original World Trade Center towers stood about two blocks west of here, and when they collapsed, the dust and debris cloud blew through the cemetery and against the church walls. The building took damage but did not fall, and the congregation reopened a few days later. Trinity functioned as one of the relief centers in the months following the attack, and the small chapel a few blocks north — St. Paul's, the older sister chapel to Trinity, completed in 1766 — served as the meal and rest station for rescue workers throughout the recovery effort.

The graveyard is open to the public during church hours, and the church itself is open every day for visitors. The lobby just inside the front doors has a small museum about the parish's history.

tours.title: Domino Park
tours.shortDescription: A former sugar refinery transformed into a public waterfront park, its industrial cranes and catwalks preserved as sculptural landmarks.
tours.longDescription: Domino Park occupies the site of the Domino Sugar Refinery, which operated on the Williamsburg waterfront from 1856 to 2004 — for most of the twentieth century, the largest sugar refinery in America. Designed by James Corner Field Operations (also responsible for the High Line) and opened in 2018, the park preserves the refinery's rusted cranes, syrup tanks, and conveyors as integrated park features. The elevated walkway follows the catwalks once used by refinery workers, offering the same view across the East River to the Manhattan skyline and the Williamsburg Bridge.

stops.title: Domino Park — Elevated Walkway
stops.caption: Former Domino Sugar Refinery site, Williamsburg waterfront
stops.transcriptText:
The cranes above you used to lift sugar.

You're on the elevated walkway at Domino Park, on the Williamsburg waterfront of the East River. To your west, across the water, the Manhattan skyline — from the spires of midtown north to the towers of the Lower East Side. Closer in, the Williamsburg Bridge crosses the river to your south. Around you, preserved as park features, are the rusted cranes, syrup tanks, conveyors, and steel beams of what used to be the Domino Sugar Refinery — for most of the twentieth century, the largest sugar refinery in America.

The refinery operated on this site from 1856 to 2004 — one hundred and forty-eight years. At its peak, it processed more than half of all sugar consumed in the United States. The sugar arrived in raw form by ship from the Caribbean — Cuba, the Dominican Republic, Puerto Rico — was unloaded at the piers behind you, refined in the buildings around you, packaged, and shipped out. Williamsburg, the neighborhood you're standing in, grew up around the refinery. The smell of cooking sugar was in the air for over a century.

The refinery closed in 2004. The site sat mostly empty for a decade. In 2012, the developer Two Trees Management bought the property and began converting the land into a mix of housing and public space.

Domino Park opened in 2018, as part of that transformation. It was designed by James Corner Field Operations — the same firm that designed the High Line. The decision to preserve the industrial artifacts — the cranes, the syrup tanks, the catwalks — was deliberate. Rather than scrub the site of its history, the designers integrated the equipment into the park itself.

What you're standing on is what happens when industrial infrastructure is treated as sculpture. The cranes that used to swing bags of raw sugar from ships now hang above a public path. The catwalks workers used to walk are part of the elevated walkway. The view is the same view workers had — the East River, the Williamsburg Bridge, the Manhattan skyline — except now you can sit down and look at it.

Walk to the edge. Look out at the water, then back at the cranes. The park is small. The history is large.

tours.title: Wave Hill
tours.shortDescription: A 28-acre public garden in the Bronx perched above the Hudson River, once rented by Mark Twain and Theodore Roosevelt's family.
tours.longDescription: Wave Hill is a 28-acre public garden in Riverdale, the Bronx, occupying a bluff above the Hudson River with unobstructed views of the New Jersey Palisades. Wave Hill House was built in 1843 and over the following century housed Theodore Roosevelt's family in the 1870s, Mark Twain in 1901 (who called it 'a house befitting the gods'), and conductor Arturo Toscanini in the 1940s. The British government leased the estate as its UN ambassador's official residence during World War II. Donated to New York City in 1960 and opened as a public garden in 1965, Wave Hill today features a flower garden, wild garden, aquatic garden, herb and dry garden, and a conservatory — all designed in the English landscape tradition with the dramatic Palisades as backdrop.

stops.title: Wave Hill Garden
stops.caption: 28-acre public garden above the Hudson, Riverdale, Bronx
stops.transcriptText:
Mark Twain rented this house for a winter.

You're at Wave Hill, a twenty-eight-acre public garden in Riverdale, the northwest corner of the Bronx, perched on a bluff above the Hudson River. From the gardens, the view stretches west across the river to the New Jersey Palisades — the same cliffs that John D. Rockefeller Jr. paid to preserve, sixty years later, for the benefit of The Cloisters museum a few miles south. Wave Hill enjoys the view by accident of geography, not philanthropy.

The estate consists of two large houses and the grounds connecting them. Wave Hill House, the older of the two, was built in 1843 by William Lewis Morris, a New York jurist, and over the following century it was occupied by a series of remarkable tenants. Theodore Roosevelt's family rented it in the 1870s, when Roosevelt was a teenager. Mark Twain leased it for the winter of 1901, and described it in a letter as 'a house befitting the gods.' Arturo Toscanini lived here in the 1940s while conducting at NBC. The British government leased the estate during the Second World War as the official residence of the United Nations ambassador.

In 1960, the property was donated to the City of New York, and in 1965 it opened as a public garden. The current grounds include a flower garden, a wild garden, an aquatic garden, a herb and dry garden, and a conservatory with seasonal exhibits — all of it designed in the English landscape tradition, with the views to the Hudson functioning as the visual anchor.

What Wave Hill does that no other public garden in New York does is integrate horticulture with one of the most dramatic natural views in the city. The Palisades, directly across the river, are an unbroken three-hundred-foot cliff face that runs for miles. From the lawns at Wave Hill, the cliffs appear close enough to touch, but there is nothing between you and them but water and air. On clear afternoons, the view is the kind that requires no commentary.

The garden is open year-round, with seasonal exhibits in the conservatory and on the grounds. It is a forty-minute trip from Manhattan by train, and the kind of place worth a half-day on a clear afternoon.

tours.title: Queens Museum
tours.shortDescription: Home to the Panorama of the City of New York — a 9,335-square-foot scale model of all five boroughs, updated continuously since 1964.
tours.longDescription: The Queens Museum occupies the former New York City Pavilion from the 1939 and 1964 World's Fairs, in Flushing Meadows Corona Park.
