The block on the north side of 139th was designed by McKim, Mead and White, with Stanford White as the partner in charge, in an Italian Renaissance and Georgian vocabulary. The opposite side, and parts of 138th, were designed by Bruce Price in Colonial Revival; the remaining side by James Brown Lord in Renaissance Revival. The result is a single integrated streetscape that reads as one composition despite three different hands.

The original tenants were white upper-middle-class New Yorkers, who proved unwilling to live in what was then a remote part of uptown Manhattan. The development failed economically within two years, and the houses were sold off as rentals.

Beginning in 1919 and 1920, the houses began to be sold to Black families — one of the first significant transfers of high-end New York real estate to Black ownership. Over the following two decades, Strivers’ Row became the most prestigious residential address for the Black professional class in Harlem. The musicians W.C. Handy and Eubie Blake lived here, along with the dancer Bill “Bojangles” Robinson, the architect Vertner Tandy, the bandleader Fletcher Henderson, and the pastor Adam Clayton Powell Sr. The nickname “Strivers’ Row” came from the Harlem community’s recognition that the people who lived here had striven to get there.

The houses are still in private residential use. The street itself, with its mature trees and original facades, is one of the best-preserved examples of late nineteenth-century row-house architecture in Manhattan.

tours.title: IAC Building
tours.shortDescription: Frank Gehry has built in dozens of cities worldwide. The IAC Building is his only completed structure in New York.
tours.longDescription: The IAC Building rises ten stories on the West Side Highway between 18th and 19th Streets — a curtain wall of white-fritted glass with bottom edges that curve upward like the hull of a sail, its asymmetrical facades twisting slightly with each story. Completed in 2007, it is the New York headquarters of Barry Diller's media holding company IAC, and it is the only finished building Frank Gehry has ever constructed in New York City. Gehry — best known for the titanium-clad Guggenheim Bilbao and the Walt Disney Concert Hall in Los Angeles — designed the sail form at Diller's request: an avid sailor, Diller wanted something that referenced the Hudson River the building faces. The white frit pattern on the glass acts as both a sun screen and a softening agent, giving the building its characteristic milky luminescence in daylight. Across the highway, the Diller architecture cluster continues: Diane von Furstenberg's headquarters by Work Architecture Company, Little Island on the Hudson, and the High Line — both of which Diller and von Furstenberg helped fund.

stops.title: IAC Building
stops.caption: Stand on the sidewalk along the West Side Highway facing the building. Walk around the curved corner at 18th Street to see how the facades shift.
stops.transcriptText:
Frank Gehry has designed buildings in dozens of cities. He's only built one in New York.

You're on the West Side Highway, between 18th and 19th Streets, looking at the IAC Building — a ten-story curtain wall of white-fritted glass, with the bottom edges curving upward like the bottom of a sail, and an asymmetrical sequence of facades that twists slightly with each story. The building was completed in 2007 and is the New York headquarters of IAC, the media holding company founded by Barry Diller — who, with his wife Diane von Furstenberg, has been one of the most consistent patrons of architecture along this stretch of the West Side.

The architect is Frank Gehry, best known for the titanium-clad Guggenheim Museum in Bilbao and the Walt Disney Concert Hall in Los Angeles. Gehry's work is unusual in being almost instantly recognizable, with its sweeping curves and sculptural forms, but the firm has never built another completed structure in New York City. The IAC Building remains, as of this writing, his only finished New York project.

The form is meant to evoke overlapping sails — Diller, an avid sailor, asked Gehry for something that referenced the Hudson River the building faces. The white frit pattern on the glass curtain wall acts as both a sun screen and a softening device, giving the building its characteristic milky luminescence in daylight and an ethereal glow at night.

Across the West Side Highway from the IAC Building is the rest of Diller's contribution to the neighborhood: Diane von Furstenberg's headquarters and showroom designed by Work Architecture Company, the new Little Island park on the Hudson, and the High Line, which Diller and von Furstenberg helped fund. Little Island and the High Line each have their own Atlas tours.

The IAC Building is an office building, not open to the public. Look at it from the sidewalk, walk around the curved corner at 18th Street to see how the facades shift, and continue along the highway for a good view of the whole Diller architecture cluster.

tours.title: The Strand Bookstore
tours.shortDescription: The Strand opened in 1927 on a block with forty-seven other bookstores. It's the only one left.
tours.longDescription: The Strand stands at the corner of Broadway and 12th Street under its red-and-white awning, outdoor carts of used books lining the sidewalk — the last surviving member of Book Row, the used-and-rare bookstore district that once stretched along Fourth Avenue from Astor Place to Union Square. At its peak in the 1920s and 30s, Book Row held forty-eight separate stores across six blocks, sustained by cheap prewar rents, proximity to NYU and Cooper Union, and a steady stream of librarians, scholars, and collectors. The Strand opened on Fourth Avenue in 1927, founded by Lithuanian-born immigrant Benjamin Bass, who specialized in used books at unusually low prices. His son Fred moved the store to this Broadway location in 1957. The other bookstores closed one by one — rising rents, mass-market paperbacks, chain stores, eventually the internet. By the early 2000s only the Strand remained. The store now claims eighteen miles of books across three floors, with a rare-books room on the third floor holding signed first editions. Still family-owned, now in its third generation, run by Fred's daughter Nancy Bass Wyden.

stops.title: The Strand Bookstore
stops.caption: Stand at the corner of Broadway and 12th Street, facing the red awning and the outdoor book carts.
stops.transcriptText:
The Strand opened in 1927 on a block with forty-seven other bookstores. They're all gone.

You're at 828 Broadway, on the corner of 12th Street, outside the Strand Bookstore — the red-and-white awning, the outdoor carts of used books, the lines of people browsing on the sidewalk. The Strand is the last surviving member of what was, for most of the twentieth century, the used-and-rare bookstore district known as Book Row.

Book Row was the stretch of Fourth Avenue between Astor Place and Union Square, two blocks east of where you're standing. At its peak in the 1920s and 30s, the area contained forty-eight separate bookstores along a six-block stretch — used, antiquarian, scholarly, specialized. It survived on cheap prewar rents, on proximity to NYU, Cooper Union, and the New School, and on the librarians, scholars, and collectors who used Book Row as their primary supply line.

The Strand opened on Fourth Avenue in 1927, founded by a Lithuanian-born immigrant named Benjamin Bass. The store specialized in used books at unusually low prices and grew rapidly. Bass passed the business to his son Fred in 1956, and Fred moved the store to this Broadway location the following year, slightly outside the by-then-declining Book Row.

The other bookstores closed one by one over the second half of the twentieth century. Rising rents, mass-market paperbacks, chain bookstores, eventually the internet — every shift in the publishing industry shrank Book Row by one or two stores, and by the early 2000s only the Strand was left.

The store now claims a stock of eighteen miles of books — a tagline used since the 1980s — across three floors, with a rare-books room on the third floor that holds signed first editions. The Strand is still family-owned, now in its third generation, run by Nancy Bass Wyden, Fred's daughter.

The carts on the sidewalk are usually the cheapest books in the store; the third floor has the most expensive.

tours.title: Church of Saint Francis
tours.shortDescription: Two and a half minutes inside Porto's most spectacular contradiction — a Franciscan church (built on a vow of poverty) whose interior is plated with 300–400 kg of Brazilian gold leaf.
tours.longDescription:
Begun in 1383 under King Fernando I and completed around 1425, the Igreja de São Francisco was raised by an order founded on a vow of poverty — and the west facade still shows it. The Gothic rose window and pointed arches sit in the deliberately plain mendicant manner, the last moment of restraint the building ever had.

Step inside and the church abandons its own founding principle. Every surface — walls, pillars, side chapels, ceiling — disappears beneath talha dourada, the carved gilded woodwork applied through the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries with somewhere between 300 and 400 kilograms of gold leaf, most of it sourced from Brazil and bankrolled by the colonial wealth the original friars could not have imagined and certainly could not have reconciled. The cumulative effect is less like being in a church than being inside a very large gold object that has been persuaded to function as one.

Two things deserve more time than visitors usually give them: the polychrome Tree of Jesse altarpiece (Retable da Árvore de Jessé) on the left of the nave, and the white stone statue of Saint Francis himself, the only figure in the interior without a gram of gold on it. Downstairs the catacombs hold a small glass window set into the nave floor above, through which you can look down at where the people who paid for all of it eventually ended up.

stops.title: Church of Saint Francis
stops.caption: On Rua do Infante Dom Henrique, facing the Gothic facade — Palácio da Bolsa to your left.
stops.transcriptText:
The Franciscans built this church, which creates a certain tension with what's inside it — because the Franciscan Order was founded on a vow of poverty.
Have a look at the exterior before you go in: you're on Rua do Infante Dom Henrique, with the Gothic facade of the church directly ahead of you, next door to the Palácio da Bolsa on your left. The west facade has a rose window and pointed arches in the restrained Gothic manner typical of mendicant orders — churches built by communities who had sworn to own nothing, designed to look like it. The Franciscans arrived in Porto in twelve twenty-three, this church was commissioned by King Fernando the First in thirteen eighty-three, and it was completed around fourteen twenty-five in the same plain, deliberately austere style, which is the last moment of restraint the building ever had.
Go in through the main entrance and give your eyes a moment to adjust.
What you're looking at — covering the walls, the pillars, the side chapels, the ceiling, essentially every surface that could receive it — is talha dourada, carved gilded woodwork applied to the interior during the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries using somewhere between three hundred and four hundred kilograms of gold leaf, most of it sourced from Brazil, where Portugal had by then built a colonial empire that generated wealth on a scale the original Franciscan friars could not have imagined and certainly could not have reconciled with their vow. The cumulative effect, standing in the nave and looking toward the altar, is less like being in a church and more like being inside a very large gold object that has been persuaded to function as one.
Now find two specific things before you move further in.
The first is the Tree of Jesse altarpiece — the Retable da Árvore de Jessé — on the left side of the nave, a polychrome carved composition showing the genealogy of Christ growing upward from Jesse's reclining figure through an elaborate branching structure of figures and foliage, which is the finest single object in the church and deserves more time than most visitors give it. The second is the white statue of Saint Francis himself, which is somewhere in the interior without a gram of gold on it, and which is considerably more striking for that reason than almost anything around it — a plain stone figure from the thirteenth century, still carrying the austerity the building abandoned three hundred years after he died.
[beat]
If you're willing to go downstairs before you leave, the catacombs beneath the church are worth the visit, not because they're particularly spectacular but because the floor of the nave above has a small glass window set into it through which you can look down at them, and there's something rather thought-provoking about standing in one of the most gilded interiors in Portugal and being able to see, through a pane of glass beneath your feet, where the people who paid for all of it eventually ended up.

tours.title: Dom Luís I Bridge
tours.shortDescription: Almost three minutes on Porto's iconic double-deck iron arch — not designed by Eiffel, but by his Belgian partner Théophile Seyrig, who entered the 1879 competition independently and beat Eiffel's own competing proposal.
tours.longDescription:
Almost everyone assumes this bridge was designed by Gustave Eiffel, which is understandable but wrong — and the true story is, if anything, more interesting. The Dom Luís I was designed by Théophile Seyrig, a Belgian engineer who had co-founded Eiffel's firm Eiffel et Cie in 1868. When the Portuguese government held an international competition in 1879 to replace the ageing Ponte Pênsil suspension bridge, Seyrig entered independently from Brussels and beat Eiffel's own proposal.

Construction began in 1881; the upper deck and main arch were completed on 30 October 1886 and the bridge opened the next day for a toll of four réis per person. What you're looking at is a double-deck parabolic iron arch — main arch spanning 172 metres across the river at the lower level, the full upper deck running 395 metres between the masonry piers at a height of 45 metres above the water. The intersecting lattice ironwork was engineered using graphic statics methods Seyrig was simultaneously publishing in academic papers in Paris, so the bridge is in part a live demonstration of structural theory its designer was working out in real time.

The upper deck was widened in 1931 and now carries the Porto Metro, so what began as a road bridge for a horse-drawn city has become, 140 years later, a metro viaduct. The previous Ponte Pênsil was not demolished to make way for the Luís I but dismantled alongside it — the civil engineering equivalent of changing the wheels on a moving vehicle, which meant Porto never lost its river crossing for a single day during the five years of construction.

stops.title: Dom Luís I Bridge
stops.caption: From the Ribeira waterfront or the Serra do Pilar terrace across the river — the bridge needs distance to read properly.
stops.transcriptText:
Almost everyone assumes this bridge was designed by Gustave Eiffel, which is understandable but wrong — and the true story is, if anything, more interesting than the mistaken version.
The bridge was designed by Théophile Seyrig, a Belgian engineer who had co-founded Eiffel's own firm, Eiffel et Cie, back in eighteen sixty-eight, which is why the two men's work looks so similar and why the confusion is so persistent. When the Portuguese government held an international competition in eighteen seventy-nine for a new bridge to replace the ageing Ponte Pênsil suspension bridge that had stood here since eighteen forty-three, Seyrig entered independently from Brussels — and beat Eiffel's own competing proposal. Construction began in eighteen eighty-one, the upper deck and main arch were completed on the thirtieth of October eighteen eighty-six, and the bridge opened the following day, with a toll of four réis per person, which in the context of what had just been built seems, in retrospect, a modest charge.
You should be somewhere with a view of the full span, either from the Ribeira waterfront or from the Serra do Pilar terrace across the river in Vila Nova de Gaia, because the bridge needs distance to read properly.
What you're looking at is a double-deck parabolic iron arch, with the main arch spanning a hundred and seventy-two metres across the river at the lower level, and the full upper deck running to three hundred and ninety-five metres between the masonry piers that anchor it to the granite banks on both sides, at a height of forty-five metres above the water — and the lattice ironwork that carries the structure, intersecting in diagonal patterns up the inside of the arch, was engineered by Seyrig using graphic statics methods that he was simultaneously publishing in academic papers in Paris, so the bridge you're looking at was, in part, a live demonstration of structural theory that its designer was working out in real time.
The upper deck was widened in nineteen thirty-one to accommodate increased pedestrian traffic, and now carries the Porto Metro across the river, so what began as a road bridge for a city that still got around by horse has become, a hundred and forty years later, a metro viaduct — a piece of infrastructure that has simply absorbed each successive definition of what crossing a river quickly is supposed to mean.
[beat]
The previous bridge, the Ponte Pênsil, was not demolished to make way for the Luís I but was dismantled during construction, with the new bridge built alongside its towers before the old one was taken down — which is the civil engineering equivalent of changing the wheels on a moving vehicle, and which meant that Porto never lost its river crossing for a single day during the five years it took to build the replacement.

tours.title: Palácio da Bolsa
tours.shortDescription: Three and a half minutes at Porto's 19th-century Stock Exchange Palace — built on the ruins of a convent that burned in 1832 and culminating in the Salão Árabe, a Moorish dream room inaugurated for the tricentenary of Camões.
tours.longDescription:
The Palácio da Bolsa was raised on the ruins of a convent that burned down during a siege — a characteristically Porto way of beginning a stock exchange. The Igreja de São Francisco survived the fire of 24 July 1832 next door; the Commercial Association of Porto received the cleared ground from Queen Maria II in 1841 and laid the first stone the following year, with the intention of announcing the city's commercial ambitions to the world.

Step inside and stop in the Pátio das Nações — the Courtyard of Nations — under Tomás Soler's octagonal iron-and-glass dome of around 1880. The flags of the nations Porto traded with in the nineteenth century run around the upper walls, turning architectural ornament into a list of business contacts. The shell was largely complete by 1850 but the interior decoration ran on until 1909, becoming an informal training ground for several generations of Portuguese craftsmen.

The room the whole building is leading you toward is the Salão Árabe, designed by Gustavo Adolfo Gonçalves e Sousa and built over eighteen years between 1862 and 1880. Every available surface is carved and gilded stucco, gold Arabic inscriptions moving across walls and ceiling, the room shifting between deep crimson, blue and gold — no structural reason whatsoever to look like a Moorish palace and every commercial reason to make visiting dignitaries feel they had arrived somewhere extraordinary. It was inaugurated on 12 June 1880, deliberately timed to the tricentenary of the death of Luís de Camões. The Golden Room nearby still hosts the Association's monthly board meeting; its display cases hold the oldest bottle of Port in the collection and the pen Queen Maria II used in 1841 to sign the building's site into existence.

stops.title: Palácio da Bolsa
stops.caption: On Rua Ferreira Borges, facing the neoclassical granite facade — Igreja de São Francisco to your right.
stops.transcriptText:
The building in front of you was raised on the ruins of a convent that burned down during a siege — which is a characteristically Porto way of beginning a stock exchange.
Take a moment to orient yourself: you're on Rua Ferreira Borges, with the neoclassical granite facade of the Palácio da Bolsa directly ahead of you and the Igreja de São Francisco to your right, the Gothic church that survived the same fire that destroyed the convent beside it on the night of the twenty-fourth of July, eighteen thirty-two. The Commercial Association of Porto received the cleared ground from Queen Maria II in eighteen forty-one and laid the first stone of this building the following year, with the intention of constructing something that would announce the city's commercial ambitions to the world — and if the exterior seems slightly soberer than what I'm about to describe inside, that contrast is entirely deliberate.
Go in through the main entrance and stop in the Pátio das Nações — the Courtyard of Nations — before moving further into the building.
The courtyard is covered with an octagonal iron-and-glass dome designed by Tomás Soler and completed around eighteen eighty, and the flags of the nations with which Porto held commercial relations in the nineteenth century run around the upper walls — which I always find rather telling, because it turns architectural ornament into a list of business contacts, and makes the ambitions of a trading city visible as a kind of institutional decoration. The main shell of the building was largely complete by eighteen fifty, but the interior decoration wasn't finished until nineteen oh nine, which means the project took nearly seven decades to complete and, in the process, became an informal training ground for several generations of Portuguese craftsmen and decorative artists who learned their trade by working on it.
Now follow the tour through to the Arab Room — the Salão Árabe — which is the room the whole building is really leading you toward.
It took eighteen years to build, between eighteen sixty-two and eighteen eighty, designed by Gustavo Adolfo Gonçalves e Sousa, who drew his inspiration from the Alhambra in Granada — every available surface covered in carved and gilded stucco, gold Arabic inscriptions moving across the walls and ceiling, the room shifting between deep crimson, blue, and gold in a way that has no structural reason whatsoever to look like a Moorish palace and every commercial reason to make visiting foreign dignitaries feel that they have arrived somewhere entirely extraordinary. The room was inaugurated on the twelfth of June, eighteen eighty, timed to coincide with the tricentennial celebrations of the death of Luís de Camões — Portugal's greatest poet — because the Association was building not just a space to impress merchants but a room that placed Porto's commercial culture deliberately inside Portugal's own sense of its literary and historical greatness.
[beat]
Before you leave, find the Golden Room, where the board of the Commercial Association still meets every month, and have a look at the display cases: they hold the oldest bottle of Port wine in the association's collection and the pen that Queen Maria II used in eighteen forty-one to sign the document granting the monastery ruins to the Association in the first place. The pen that made the building possible is kept inside the building it made possible — which is either very tidy record-keeping, or a rather elegant piece of institutional self-awareness, and on balance I think it's both.

tours.title: Porto Cathedral
tours.shortDescription: Three and a half minutes at Penaventosa Hill — a Romanesque-bones cathedral founded by the mother of Portugal's first king, refaced over eight centuries with Gothic cloisters, Nasoni's loggia, and azulejos of Ovid and the Song of Solomon.
tours.longDescription:
The Sé do Porto was already standing when Portugal became a country, because it was founded by the woman whose son became its first king. Dona Teresa, mother of Afonso Henriques, commissioned the original church on Penaventosa Hill in the early twelfth century. The structure was Romanesque in its bones — thick granite walls, small windows, two square bell towers with crenellated arches that made the facade read more like a fortified military building than a place of worship, which on the twelfth-century Iberian peninsula was not an unreasonable precaution.

What you're looking at now is eight centuries of addition, renovation, and stylistic overlay: Gothic cloisters, Baroque altars, Nasoni's loggia, and a 1930s restoration that tried to reconcile everything that had accumulated. The only original feature of the main facade still in place is the Romanesque rose window, set within a crenellated arch above the entrance — a circle of carved stone that has presided over everything added around it, including Nicolau Nasoni's elegant Baroque loggia of 1736 on the north side, which sits with characteristic Nasoni ease against the older stone.

Inside, turn toward the cloister. Begun in 1385 — the same year as the Battle of Aljubarrota that secured Portuguese independence from Castile — its walls were lined in the eighteenth century with azulejo panels by Valentim de Almeida depicting scenes from the Song of Solomon and from Ovid's Metamorphoses. Ovid on the walls of a fourteenth-century cloister rewards a moment's thought. The main chapel's silver altarpiece of the Sacrament was hidden behind a layer of plaster during the Napoleonic invasion to prevent it being seized or melted down, and rediscovered after the French left. Dom João I and Philippa of Lancaster were married here in 1387 — the building has been accumulating significant events for almost as long as it has been accumulating architectural styles.

stops.title: Porto Cathedral
stops.caption: From the terrace in front of the facade — the rose window above the main entrance is the only original twelfth-century feature still in place.
stops.transcriptText:
The building in front of you was already standing when Portugal became a country, because it was founded by the woman whose son became the country's first king — which is a reasonable way for a cathedral to begin.
Dona Teresa, mother of Afonso Henriques, commissioned the original church on Penaventosa Hill in the early twelfth century, and the structure that rose from that founding was Romanesque in its bones: thick granite walls, small windows, two square bell towers with crenellated arches that gave the whole facade the appearance of a fortified military building rather than a place of worship, which in the Iberian peninsula of the twelfth century was not an unreasonable precaution. What you're looking at now is the result of eight centuries of addition, renovation, and stylistic overlay, during which Gothic cloisters were added, Baroque altars were inserted, Nasoni arrived with his loggia, and the 1930s brought a restoration that tried to reconcile everything that had accumulated — so the cathedral is less a building from one period than a record of what people in each successive century thought a cathedral ought to look like.
Before you go inside, stop and look at the façade from the terrace.
The only original feature of the main facade that has survived every subsequent intervention is the Romanesque rose window, set within a crenellated arch above the main entrance — a circle of carved stone that has been here since the twelfth century, presiding over everything that was added around it and beneath it, including Nasoni's elegant Baroque loggia on the north side, which he added in seventeen thirty-six and which sits with characteristic Nasoni ease against the older stone, as though it had always been part of the composition rather than an eighteenth-century afterthought.
Go in through the main entrance and then turn toward the cloister.
The Gothic cloister was begun in thirteen eighty-five — the same year as the Battle of Aljubarrota, when Portugal secured its independence from Castile — and its walls were lined in the eighteenth century with azulejo tile panels painted by Valentim de Almeida, which depict two subjects that sit in interesting company in a cathedral cloister: scenes from the Song of Solomon, the biblical love poem, and scenes from Ovid's Metamorphoses, the Roman mythological epic. Ovid on the walls of a fourteenth-century church cloister is the kind of detail that rewards a moment's thought, because it reflects the particular Baroque sensibility that saw no contradiction between classical learning and Christian devotion, and which treated the walls of a sacred building as a reasonable place to depict a Roman poet's account of gods turning into swans.
[beat]
In the main chapel, the silver altarpiece of the Sacrament — one of the most valuable objects in the cathedral — was hidden behind a layer of plaster during the Napoleonic invasion of the early nineteenth century, to prevent it from being seized or melted down, and rediscovered after the French had left, which is the kind of story that tends to be filed under institutional wisdom but might equally be filed under the experience of having had your cathedral occupied by a foreign army and taking sensible precautions. Dom João I and Philippa of Lancaster were married here in thirteen eighty-seven, two years after the cloister was begun, which means the building has been accumulating significant events for almost as long as it has been accumulating architectural styles.

tours.title: Ribeira District
tours.shortDescription: Three minutes on Porto's UNESCO waterfront — eight centuries of medieval river city stacked up the Douro bank, where Henry the Navigator was born in 1394 in what was, at the time, a municipal tax office.
tours.longDescription:
The buildings of the Ribeira are so steep that some can be entered at street level on one side and from the roof on the street above — a reasonable summary of what the district has always been: a place that organises itself around the river first and worries about conventional urbanism second. You're on the north bank of the Douro, in the quarter that effectively made Porto a UNESCO World Heritage city when the designation was granted in 1996 — not because the district was preserved in amber but because eight hundred years of continuous commercial occupation produced a layered urban fabric the committee considered irreplaceable.

Narrow granite townhouses painted in ochre, terracotta and blue stack up the hillside so steeply that conventional street geometry becomes impossible. The Cais da Ribeira — the quayside — is where merchants loaded barrels of port wine onto flat-bottomed rabelo boats for transport downstream to the Atlantic, a trade that ran for centuries and shaped not only this waterfront but the entire Douro Valley above it. The lodges across the river in Vila Nova de Gaia, visible from where you stand, were the storage and ageing warehouses for the same wine, positioned on the south bank because the cooler microclimate suited the barrels.

A short walk to the right is the Casa do Infante, built in 1325 as the customs house for the port and named after Infante Dom Henrique — Henry the Navigator — who was born in this building in 1394, which makes it the birthplace of the man who launched Portugal's Age of Discovery from what was, at the time of his birth, a municipal tax office. The Guindais funicular, which has been descending the cliff face from Batalha to the quayside in various forms since 1891, drops sixty-one metres in three minutes; the walk back up through the medieval alleys rewards getting slightly lost.

stops.title: Ribeira District
stops.caption: On the Cais da Ribeira, north bank of the Douro — Vila Nova de Gaia's port-wine lodges across the water.
stops.transcriptText:
The buildings you're walking past are so steep that some of them can be entered at street level on one side and from the roof on the street above, which is a reasonable summary of what the Ribeira has always been: a place that organises itself around the river first and worries about conventional urbanism second.
You're on the north bank of the Douro, in the compact quarter that effectively made Porto a UNESCO World Heritage city when the designation was granted in nineteen ninety-six — not because the district was preserved in amber but because it accumulated, over roughly eight hundred years of continuous commercial occupation, a layered urban fabric that the committee considered irreplaceable. The buildings are narrow granite townhouses painted in ochre, terracotta, and blue, stacked up the hillside so steeply that the topography makes conventional street geometry impossible, and if you look up the hill behind you, what you're seeing is essentially a medieval solution to the problem of building a lot of housing on a very steep riverbank, which turns out to produce exactly the kind of dense, vertical, irregularly-beautiful neighbourhood that people now travel considerable distances to walk through.
Before you explore the alleys, look at the waterfront in front of you.
The Cais da Ribeira — the quayside — is where merchants loaded barrels of port wine onto flat-bottomed rabelo boats for transport downstream to the Atlantic, a trade that ran for centuries and that shaped not only this waterfront but the entire Douro Valley above it, where the vine terraces were planted specifically to produce wine for export through this quay. The lodges across the river in Vila Nova de Gaia, visible from where you're standing, were the storage and ageing warehouses for that same wine, positioned on the south bank because the cooler, slightly more stable microclimate there was better for the barrels — so the trade route that made Porto wealthy had its loading point here and its maturation point directly across the water, connected by the river that ran between them.
Now find the Casa do Infante, which is on the waterfront a short walk to your right.
It was built in thirteen twenty-five as a customs house for the port, and it is named after Infante Dom Henrique — Henry the Navigator — who was born in this building in thirteen ninety-four, which makes it the birthplace of the man who launched Portugal's Age of Discovery and, in doing so, changed the direction of global history from a building that was, at the time of his birth, a municipal tax office. It is now a museum and the headquarters of Porto's historical archive, which is either a promotion or a demotion depending on your view of how history should be housed.
[beat]
The Guindais funicular, which descends sixty-one metres down the cliff face from the Batalha area to the quayside in three minutes, has been making that journey in various forms since eighteen ninety-one — and if you haven't come down that way, it's worth knowing that the walk back up through the medieval alleys is one of those urban experiences that rewards getting slightly lost, because the streets that don't lead anywhere obvious tend to be the ones that lead somewhere worth finding.

tours.title: São Bento Station
tours.shortDescription: Three minutes inside Porto's 1916 train station — twenty thousand Jorge Colaço azulejos covering the entry hall, named with characteristic Portuguese tact after the Benedictine monastery the railway replaced.
tours.longDescription:
The name of the station is the first piece of history it offers: São Bento — Saint Benedict — refers to the Benedictine monastery that stood on this site for three centuries before the last nun died in 1892 and the building was demolished to make way for the railway. The station was named, with a certain Portuguese tact, after the institution it replaced.

King Carlos I laid the foundation stone in 1900. The architect was José Marques da Silva, a Porto native trained at the École des Beaux-Arts in Paris, who would go on to reshape much of the city's early-twentieth-century fabric. The station was inaugurated in 1916, by which point the entry hall had already been covered over the preceding eleven years with twenty thousand azulejo tiles painted by Jorge Colaço — the fact that makes São Bento something rather more than a working railway terminus.

The hall is covered in two registers. Large blue-and-white panels at eye level depict specific historical episodes — the Battle of Valdevez in 1140, Egas Moniz before Alfonso VII of Castile, the 1387 arrival of João I and Philippa of Lancaster for their royal wedding, the Conquest of Ceuta in 1415. Above them a polychrome frieze runs toward the ceiling tracing the history of transport in Portugal up to the arrival of the railway. Colaço, born in Tangier in 1868 to a Portuguese diplomat, worked almost entirely in the azulejo tradition; figurative panels on this scale almost never appear in a secular public building — the genre grew out of ecclesiastical patronage, and the decision to treat a railway station as worthy of the same ambition is either civic self-confidence or a very good accident. The station is still fully operational, with trains leaving for the Douro Valley, Braga, Guimarães and the coast.

stops.title: São Bento Station
stops.caption: Just inside the main entrance — the entry hall is a single coherent artwork, floor to ceiling.
stops.transcriptText:
The name of this station is the first piece of history it offers you, because São Bento — Saint Benedict — refers to the Benedictine monastery that stood on this site for three centuries before the last nun died in eighteen ninety-two and the building was demolished to make way for the railway, which means that the station was named, with a certain Portuguese tact, after the institution it replaced.
King Carlos I laid the foundation stone in nineteen hundred, the architect was José Marques da Silva — a Porto native who had trained at the École des Beaux-Arts in Paris and who would go on to reshape much of the city's early-twentieth-century fabric — and the station was inaugurated in nineteen sixteen, by which point the hall you're about to enter had already been covered, over the preceding eleven years, with twenty thousand azulejo tiles painted by Jorge Colaço, which is the fact that makes São Bento something rather more than a functioning railway terminus.
Go inside and stop as soon as you're through the door.
The hall is covered on every wall with tile panels in two registers: the large blue-and-white panels at eye level, which depict specific historical episodes in a narrative sequence — the Battle of Valdevez in eleven forty, the meeting of the knight Egas Moniz with Alfonso VII of Castile, the arrival of King João I and Philippa of Lancaster in Porto in thirteen eighty-seven for their royal wedding, the Conquest of Ceuta in fourteen fifteen — and above them, running in a polychrome frieze toward the ceiling, a sequence depicting the history of transport in Portugal from its primitive origins up to the moment when the railway arrived and someone decided to commemorate the whole story in ceramic on the walls of a station. The scale of it is worth taking a moment to absorb before you start reading the individual panels, because the instinct to move immediately to the nearest scene tends to make you miss the fact that the entire hall is, from floor to ceiling, a single coherent artwork.
Colaço was born in Tangier in eighteen sixty-eight, the son of a Portuguese diplomat, and spent his career working almost entirely in the azulejo tradition — and what he did here, which the writer on tiles Olá Daniela put well, is something that large figurative azulejo panels almost never do, which is appear in a secular public building: normally you find work on this scale in churches, because the genre grew out of ecclesiastical patronage, and the decision to treat a railway station as a space worthy of the same ambition is either a piece of civic self-confidence or a very good accident, depending on how much you credit the committee that commissioned it.
[beat]
The station is still fully operational — trains leave from here to the Douro Valley, to Braga, to Guimarães, and along the coast — and the combination of commuters moving through the hall and tourists standing still in the middle of it produces, most mornings, exactly the kind of productive confusion that a building like this deserves, where the people who know where they're going and the people who have stopped to look are equally at home in the same space.

tours.title: Torre dos Clérigos
tours.shortDescription: Three and a quarter minutes at Porto's 75m baroque bell tower — meant to be one of two, built by Nicolau Nasoni between 1732 and 1763, who agreed to work for free and is buried in the crypt below.
tours.longDescription:
The tower was supposed to be one of two, which is worth knowing before you look at it — it changes the way the single tower reads against the skyline, not as a building designed to stand alone but as one half of a symmetrical composition that was never completed, leaving Nicolau Nasoni's Baroque ambition visible in the asymmetry it produced.

Nasoni was an Italian architect born in Tuscany in 1691 who arrived in Porto in 1725 and spent the rest of his life here, reshaping the city's Baroque fabric more thoroughly than any other architect of his period. The Brotherhood of the Clérigos — a charitable institution supporting the poorest clergy in Porto — commissioned the church and tower from him over a period that stretched from the foundation stone in 1732 to the iron cross placed at the summit in July 1763, by which point Nasoni was in his seventies and had agreed to work without remuneration, presumably because he believed in what he was building. He is buried in the church crypt at his own request; the exact location of his grave within it is not known.

Seventy-five metres of granite in six floors, Baroque ornament unwinding upward, forty-nine bells in the belfry at the top — used in the eighteenth century to announce ship arrivals in the Ribeira, to signal noon by mortar fire, to ring royal births and deaths, and at night as a visual reference for sailors navigating the Douro, with fires lit near the top. The Igreja dos Clérigos on the ground floor was one of the first Baroque churches in Portugal to adopt an elliptical floor plan, and the oval nave produces acoustic properties the two pipe organs flanking the main altar were specifically designed to exploit. The tower remained the tallest building in Portugal from its completion in 1763 until the late nineteenth century, and is still the tallest structure in Porto's historic quarter.

stops.title: Torre dos Clérigos
stops.caption: From the small praça at the base — read the tower from the ground up before climbing the 240 steps.
stops.transcriptText:
The tower you're looking at was supposed to be one of two, which is worth knowing before you look at it, because it changes the way the single tower reads against the skyline — not as a building that was designed to stand alone, but as one half of a symmetrical composition that was never completed, presumably for financial reasons, leaving Nicolau Nasoni's Baroque ambition visible in the asymmetry it produced.
Nasoni was an Italian architect born in Tuscany in sixteen ninety-one who arrived in Porto in seventeen twenty-five and spent the rest of his life here, leaving a body of work that reshaped the Baroque fabric of the city more thoroughly than any other single architect of his period — and the Brotherhood of the Clérigos, a charitable institution founded to support the poorest clergy in Porto, commissioned the church and tower complex from him over a period that stretched from the laying of the foundation stone in seventeen thirty-two to the placement of the iron cross at the summit in July of seventeen sixty-three, by which point Nasoni was in his seventies and had, by the early stages of the project, agreed to work without remuneration, presumably because he believed in what he was building. He is buried in the church crypt, at his own request, though the exact location of his grave within it is not known.
Stand back from the base if you can, and read the tower from the ground up.
Seventy-five metres of granite in six floors, with Baroque ornament — statues of saints, prominent cornices, balustrades, the spiral form unwinding upward — and forty-nine bells in the belfry at the top, which were used in the eighteenth century for a range of civic functions that tell you a great deal about what a tower like this meant to a city before it had any other way of communicating at scale: the arrival of ships in the Ribeira was announced from here, noon was signalled by firing a mortar from the steeple, royal births and royal deaths were rung out on the bells, and at night sailors navigating the Douro used the tower as a visual reference, with fires lit near the top to mark its position in the dark, so the building was simultaneously a church, a clock, a lighthouse, and a broadcasting system for a city that had no other.
Before or after you climb the two hundred and forty steps to the top, go into the church on the ground floor and look at the plan.
The Igreja dos Clérigos was one of the first Baroque churches in Portugal to adopt an elliptical floor plan, which Nasoni derived from his knowledge of Italian Baroque precedents, and the oval nave produces acoustic properties that the two pipe organs flanking the main altar were specifically designed to exploit — so the interior is not only visually unusual but was conceived as a musical instrument, which is the kind of ambition that tends to announce itself rather quietly until you happen to be inside it when someone begins to play.
[beat]
The tower remained the tallest building in Portugal from its completion in seventeen sixty-three until the late nineteenth century, and it is still the tallest structure in Porto's historic quarter, which means that for most of the city's modern history the point on the skyline that told you where you were, whether you were arriving by river or by road or simply looking up from the Ribeira, was the top of a building that one Italian architect agreed to build for nothing because the Brotherhood asked him nicely and he thought it was worth doing.

tours.title: Ford Foundation Building

stops.title: Ford Foundation Building
stops.caption: Stand at 320 East 43rd Street between First and Second Avenues, looking at the Cor-Ten frame and glass corner.
stops.transcriptText:
This is an office building with a twelve-story garden inside it.

You're at 320 East 43rd Street, between First and Second Avenues, looking at the Ford Foundation Building — designed by the firm of Kevin Roche, John Dinkeloo and Associates and completed in 1967. Before the garden — and we'll get to the garden — give the outside a moment, because it would be worth stopping for even if there were nothing behind the glass. The frame is Cor-Ten steel, deliberately left to weather to a deep russet brown, set against piers of brown granite, with two great glass walls meeting at the building's southern and western corner. Where most nineteen-sixties office buildings reached for sleek anonymity — the glass box, repeated floor after floor — this one is warm, heavy, and unmistakably itself, as much a piece of civic architecture as a place to work.

And then there's what it's built around. The southwest corner — the part you can see straight through the glass — is a single enclosed garden, one hundred and thirty feet tall and roughly a quarter of an acre in plan, with mature trees, terraced plantings, a small pool, and a glass roof open to the sky. The garden was designed by the modernist landscape architect Dan Kiley and conceived as an indoor public park for the building's employees and visitors. Every office in the building — about three hundred of them, across ten floors — opens onto the atrium, with desks and meeting rooms wrapped around the garden like a giant glass-walled bookshelf.

The architects, Kevin Roche and John Dinkeloo, had taken over the practice of Eero Saarinen after Saarinen's death in 1961, and the Ford Foundation Building was the first major project they completed under their own names. The design was meant to embody the Ford Foundation's institutional values of openness and shared mission — rather than retreat into private offices facing outward, every employee would face the same shared garden, and everyone in the building would be able to see everyone else at work.

[beat]

The building was an immediate critical sensation, and it became one of the most copied corporate types of the late twentieth century. The dramatic enclosed atrium — floors of offices opening onto it — became the model for hundreds of later buildings, including the John Portman hotels of the 1970s and 80s and countless variants since. Many of those imitations are larger, glassier, and more theatrical, yet none quite recover the specific calm of Kiley's planted garden, which is unusual among atriums in being more park than spectacle.

The Ford Foundation Building was designated a New York City landmark in 1997, and reopened in 2018 as the Ford Foundation Center for Social Justice after a major restoration by the firm Gensler, with the atrium garden carefully brought back.

The atrium is open to the public during weekday business hours, free of charge — the Ford Foundation has kept, throughout the building's history, the original 1967 decision that the garden be available to anyone who walks in.

tours.longDescription:
At 320 East 43rd Street stands the Ford Foundation Building — Kevin Roche, John Dinkeloo & Associates, 1967 — an office building built around a twelve-story enclosed garden. The frame is Cor-Ten steel, deliberately left to weather to a deep russet brown, set against piers of brown granite, with two great glass walls meeting at the southern-and-western corner. The southwest corner is a single enclosed garden, 130 feet tall and roughly a quarter of an acre in plan, with mature trees, terraced plantings, a small pool, and a glass roof open to the sky. The garden was designed by the modernist landscape architect Dan Kiley and conceived as an indoor public park; every one of the building's roughly 300 offices opens onto the atrium, with desks and meeting rooms wrapped around the garden like a giant glass-walled bookshelf.

Roche and Dinkeloo had taken over Eero Saarinen's practice after his death in 1961, and the Ford Foundation Building was their first major project under their own names. It was an immediate critical sensation and became the model for hundreds of later atrium buildings — the John Portman hotels of the 1970s and 80s and countless variants since — though few recover the specific calm of Kiley's planted garden. Designated a NYC landmark in 1997 and reopened in 2018 as the Ford Foundation Center for Social Justice after a Gensler restoration. The atrium is open to the public during weekday business hours, free of charge — kept open since 1967.
tours.shortDescription: Kevin Roche & John Dinkeloo's 1967 modernist landmark at 320 East 43rd — Cor-Ten steel frame around Dan Kiley's twelve-story enclosed garden atrium, the model for hundreds of imitators.

tours.title: Tudor City Greens

stops.title: Tudor City Greens
stops.caption: Stand in either of the two small parks on East 42nd Street at Tudor City Place, framed by Tudor Revival apartment towers.
stops.transcriptText:
Almost every apartment window in the buildings around you faces these parks, not the East River. There's a reason.

You're in Tudor City Greens, the two small parks on either side of East 42nd Street between First and Second Avenues, just west of the United Nations. The twelve buildings surrounding the parks make up Tudor City, one of the first residential skyscraper complexes in the United States, built between 1925 and 1932 by the developer Fred F. French and designed primarily by the architect H. Douglas Ives. The style is Tudor Revival — Gothic arches, gargoyles, leaded glass, half-timbering — all applied to twenty-five-story apartment buildings.

The parks were designed as the front yards of the complex, which is why the buildings around you face inward toward the gardens with their largest windows and most ornate facades. The river side, where the United Nations now stands, was at the time of construction a strip of slaughterhouses, gas plants, coal yards, and the worst tenement slums in Manhattan — and Tudor City was deliberately designed to turn its back on that view. The apartments rented in the late 1920s with the promise that residents would never have to look at the river.

That promise aged badly. In 1947, the United Nations chose the slaughterhouse site for its new headquarters, and by 1952 the back of Tudor City — its lowest-rent, fewest-windowed side — looked out at one of the most famous diplomatic skylines in the world. Apartment values shifted accordingly.

[beat]

The parks themselves nearly didn't survive. In 1972, the real estate magnate Harry Helmsley, who had acquired most of Tudor City a few years earlier, announced plans to demolish both Greens and replace them with two new apartment towers. The Tudor City residents organized in opposition, joined by city preservationists, and the campaign to save the parks lasted more than a decade. The parks were sold to the Trust for Public Land in 1986 and transferred to the City of New York in 1988. They are now operated by a nonprofit called Tudor City Greens, Inc.

Both parks are small — about three-quarters of an acre between them — but they sit on a bluff above the rest of midtown, and the view east from either one runs straight to the East River. Forty-second Street passes underneath you, in the cut where it slices through Murray Hill before reaching the river.

tours.longDescription:
Tudor City Greens are the two small parks on either side of East 42nd Street between First and Second Avenues, just west of the United Nations. The twelve buildings around them make up Tudor City, one of the first residential skyscraper complexes in the United States, built 1925–1932 by the developer Fred F. French and designed primarily by the architect H. Douglas Ives — Tudor Revival (Gothic arches, gargoyles, leaded glass, half-timbering) applied to twenty-five-story apartment buildings. The parks were designed as the complex's front yards, which is why the buildings face inward toward them with their largest windows and most ornate facades. The river side, where the UN now stands, was at the time of construction a strip of slaughterhouses, gas plants, coal yards, and the worst tenement slums in Manhattan, and Tudor City was deliberately built to turn its back on it. Apartments rented in the late 1920s with the promise that residents would never have to look at the river.

That promise aged badly: by 1952 the back of Tudor City looked out at one of the most famous diplomatic skylines in the world. The parks themselves nearly didn't survive a 1972 Harry Helmsley demolition proposal — residents and city preservationists organized in opposition for more than a decade. The parks were sold to the Trust for Public Land in 1986 and transferred to NYC in 1988; they are now operated by the nonprofit Tudor City Greens, Inc. Both parks together cover roughly three-quarters of an acre on a bluff above midtown, with views east straight to the East River; 42nd Street passes underneath in the Murray Hill cut.
tours.shortDescription: Two small Tudor Revival-framed private parks on East 42nd at Tudor City Place — front yards of Fred F. French's 1928 residential skyscraper complex, saved from demolition in the 1970s–80s and city-owned since 1988.

tours.title: United Nations Headquarters

stops.title: United Nations Headquarters
stops.caption: Stand on the west side of First Avenue, looking east across the street at the Secretariat tower and the General Assembly slab.
stops.transcriptText:
This is the closest you can stand to a piece of land that doesn't belong to any country.

You're on the west side of First Avenue, looking east across the street at the United Nations Headquarters. The campus sits on eighteen acres of what was, until the late nineteen-forties, slaughterhouses and tenements along the East River — Turtle Bay at its grimmest. John D. Rockefeller Jr. bought the land and gave it to the new organization, and once the deal was done, the site stopped being part of New York, or even the United States. The ground in front of you is international territory.

Two buildings define what you're seeing. To your left, the long, low slab with the gently curved roof and the shallow dome is the General Assembly building — the hall where every member nation gets one seat and one vote. To the right, the tall, thin tower of green glass and white marble is the Secretariat, thirty-nine stories of offices, finished in 1952. When it went up, that flat glass curtain wall was a genuine shock; almost nothing in the city looked like it yet. The whole International Style of corporate Manhattan — the glass box that would define the next half-century — arguably starts here.

The design came out of an international committee, which is exactly as fraught as it sounds. The American architect Wallace Harrison ran it, but the two figures who mattered most were Le Corbusier, the Swiss-French father of modernism, and Oscar Niemeyer, the young Brazilian who would later build Brasília. Niemeyer's sketch and Le Corbusier's scheme were eventually fused into the design that got built — and Le Corbusier, feeling robbed of credit, never quite forgave any of them.

[beat]

It helps to remember what this was supposed to be. In 1945 the world had just finished killing sixty million people, and the United Nations was the enormous, fragile bet that it never needed to happen again — that nations could be talked out of war across a table instead of a battlefield. This campus is that bet rendered in glass and marble: the postwar dream of world government, given a skyline. Whether the dream worked is a conversation that never ends. But the architecture still says it plainly — open, transparent, deliberately unlike a fortress.

Stay a moment and watch the flags. There's one for every member nation, and they fly in alphabetical order — which means the lineup quietly rearranges itself every time the membership of the world changes.

tours.longDescription:
The United Nations Headquarters sits on eighteen acres of former Turtle Bay slaughterhouses and tenements that John D. Rockefeller Jr. bought and gave to the new organization in the late 1940s — once the deal was done, the site stopped being part of New York or the United States. The ground is international territory. Two buildings define the campus. The long, low slab with the gently curved roof and shallow dome is the General Assembly building — the hall where every member nation gets one seat and one vote. The tall, thin tower of green glass and white marble is the Secretariat, thirty-nine stories of offices, finished in 1952. That flat glass curtain wall was a genuine shock when it went up; almost nothing in the city looked like it yet. The whole International Style of corporate Manhattan — the glass box that would define the next half-century — arguably starts here.

The design came out of an international committee — Wallace Harrison ran it, but Le Corbusier and the young Brazilian Oscar Niemeyer (who would later build Brasília) shaped what got built; Le Corbusier, feeling robbed of credit, never quite forgave any of them. It helps to remember what this was supposed to be: in 1945 the world had just finished killing sixty million people, and the UN was the enormous, fragile bet that it never needed to happen again — that nations could be talked out of war across a table instead of a battlefield. The campus is that bet rendered in glass and marble. Watch the flags on First Avenue: one for every member nation, flown in alphabetical order, the lineup quietly rearranging itself every time the membership of the world changes.
tours.shortDescription: The 1952 international-territory campus on the East River — Wallace Harrison led the international committee; Le Corbusier and Oscar Niemeyer shaped the Secretariat tower whose green-glass curtain wall launched the International Style.

tours.title: Avenida dos Aliados
tours.shortDescription: Three and a quarter minutes on Porto's main civic avenue — a Barry Parker layout from 1915 that demolished an entire neighbourhood to clear the ground, then handed the architecture to French-trained locals who ignored Parker's English restraint entirely.
tours.longDescription:
The Avenida dos Aliados is Porto's living room — the wide granite esplanade running north from Praça da Liberdade up to the Câmara Municipal, where New Year's Eve gathers, where FC Porto's championship parades end, and where the full civic ambition of the early twentieth century still surrounds you in two rows of Beaux-Arts and Art Nouveau granite. The avenue's origin is more contested than the buildings let on.

A British architect and urban planner named Barry Parker drew up the plan in 1915, and it required the demolition of the entire Laranjal neighbourhood — streets, buildings, the old Town Hall of 1816 — to clear the ground. Parker proposed a sober, neoclassical layout with a landscaped median and two-storey buildings, the English restraint of someone who had spent his career on garden cities. Porto's municipal architects, trained mostly in Paris, were unimpressed: Parker's plan was accepted as an urban layout but the architecture along it was handed to local designers, which is why the avenue reads Parisian rather than English and why Parker's name appears nowhere on it.

At the northern end, the City Hall (Correia da Silva, finished by Carlos Ramos, 1920–1955) survived a mid-construction proposal that the whole thing be demolished and started again on the grounds that the Beaux-Arts style had become old-fashioned. One floor was cut from the 70m central tower so it would not exceed the Clérigos church a few blocks away. The central esplanade itself was a landscaped garden until 2006, when Álvaro Siza and Eduardo Souto de Moura replaced it with the granite cobblestone you're standing on — granite because that's what Porto is built from.

stops.title: Avenida dos Aliados
stops.caption: On the central esplanade — look both ways: São Bento Station south, City Hall tower north.
stops.transcriptText:
An entire neighbourhood was demolished to make this avenue — and then the city largely ignored the architect who designed it and built something rather more flamboyant instead.
Right. You should be somewhere on the central esplanade of the Avenida dos Aliados, the wide granite-paved pedestrian strip that runs north from Praça da Liberdade up toward the City Hall at the top of the slope — and the best way to read the avenue is to stand on the central axis and look both ways along it: south toward São Bento Station and north toward the City Hall tower, with the two rows of Beaux-Arts and Art Nouveau granite buildings on either side framing the view. This is Porto's living room, as the city calls it, the place where New Year's Eve is celebrated, where FC Porto's championship victories spill into the streets, and where the full civic ambition of the early twentieth century is still visible in the buildings surrounding you.
Now, the avenue's origin is more contested than it looks.
The plan was drawn up by a British architect and urban planner named Barry Parker in nineteen fifteen, and it required the demolition of an entire neighbourhood — the Laranjal — along with its streets, its buildings, and the old Town Hall, which had stood on Praça da Liberdade since eighteen sixteen. Parker proposed a relatively sober, neoclassical design with a landscaped median, two-storey buildings, and the English restraint of someone who had spent his career building garden cities. Porto's municipal architects, who had mostly trained in Paris and had strong views about what a grand avenue should look like, were not particularly impressed by English restraint — so Parker's plan was accepted as an urban layout but the architecture along it was handed to a generation of local designers whose buildings are considerably more ornamental and exuberant than anything Parker had in mind, which is why the avenue looks Parisian rather than English, and why Barry Parker's name is not prominently displayed anywhere on it.
Look at the City Hall at the northern end of the avenue, which took thirty-five years and two architects to complete.
Designed by Correia da Silva and finished with modifications by Carlos Ramos, the building was constructed between nineteen twenty and nineteen fifty-five — and during construction, city officials at one point recommended demolishing the whole thing and starting again, on the grounds that the Beaux-Arts style had become old-fashioned. It survived, with one adjustment that tells you something useful about Porto: a floor was removed from the seventy-metre central tower so that the building would not be taller than the Clérigos church, several blocks away, because the Clérigos tower had been the tallest structure in Porto for nearly two centuries and the municipality, apparently, felt that a town hall was not the right thing to replace it.
[beat]
The central esplanade beneath your feet was originally a landscaped garden with vegetation and pergolas, which was removed in two thousand and six and replaced with the granite cobblestone surface you're standing on now — a redesign carried out by Álvaro Siza and Eduardo Souto de Moura, who have between them shaped more of Porto's contemporary fabric than any other living architects, and who chose granite because it is what Porto is built from and what Porto has always been built from, which is the kind of decision that looks obvious only after someone makes it.

tours.title: Casa do Infante
tours.shortDescription: Three minutes at Porto's oldest building — a 1325 royal customs house built directly on top of a Roman villa, where Henry the Navigator was most likely born in 1394, and which then spent the next five centuries as a tax office.
tours.longDescription:
The Casa do Infante is the oldest building in Porto, dating from 1325, and it sits on Praça do Infante Dom Henrique in the Ribeira a short walk from the Church of São Francisco on one side and the Palácio da Bolsa on the other. Four levels of granite windows above a grand arched entrance carry the royal coat of arms; a Neo-Manueline commemorative stone above the main door was placed there in 1894, five hundred years after the event it records.

King Afonso IV founded it in 1325 as the royal customs house — the Alfândega Régia — built specifically to control and tax goods arriving by river from northern Europe and Venice, and constructed on top of the remains of a Roman villa that the fourteenth-century builders either didn't know about or didn't consider relevant. Around 1370, Fernando I added a royal mint, and Porto's coins were produced here continuously from 1370 until 1721 — three and a half centuries of coinage from a building that was simultaneously handling customs, storing royal merchandise, and providing living quarters for Crown officials in the only royal building Porto possessed. Excavations in the 1990s, during the conversion into the current museum and municipal archive, uncovered fourth- and fifth-century Roman mosaic floor and foundations in situ under glass; you can stand in a fourteenth-century customs house and look down through the floor at the Roman city underneath.

The name Casa do Infante comes from an oral tradition that Henry the Navigator was born here on 4 March 1394, when his parents — King João I and Queen Philippa of Lancaster — were in Porto and this customs house was the only royal building in the city, and therefore the only plausible location for a royal birth. Henry went on to sponsor the Portuguese expeditions that opened the Atlantic and fundamentally changed the world's geography. The building where he was most likely born spent the following five centuries as a warehouse and tax office, which is the kind of biographical footnote that tends to be left out of the heroic versions of the story.

stops.title: Casa do Infante
stops.caption: On Praça do Infante Dom Henrique — the four-storey granite facade with the Neo-Manueline commemorative stone above the arched entrance.
stops.transcriptText:
This building was a customs house for over five centuries, and then it turned out that the most important thing to have happened here was a birth.
Right. You're on Praça do Infante Dom Henrique, in the Ribeira, with the facade of the Casa do Infante directly in front of you — four levels of granite windows above a grand arched entrance marked with the royal coat of arms, and a Neo-Manueline commemorative stone above the main door that was placed there in eighteen ninety-four, which is to say five hundred years after the event it records. The building is the oldest in Porto, dating from thirteen twenty-five, and it sits a short walk from the Church of São Francisco on one side and the Palácio da Bolsa on the other, in the cluster of buildings that defines the commercial and civic heart of the medieval waterfront.
Now, the building's layered history is the real reason to come inside, because it has been more things than its current appearance suggests.
It was founded in thirteen twenty-five by King Afonso IV as the royal customs house — the Alfândega Régia — built specifically to control and tax the goods arriving by river from northern Europe and Venice, and it was constructed on top of the remains of a Roman villa, which the fourteenth-century builders either didn't know about or simply didn't consider relevant. Around thirteen seventy, King Fernando the First added a royal mint to the complex, and Porto's coins were produced in this building continuously from thirteen seventy until seventeen twenty-one, which is three hundred and fifty years of coinage from a building that was simultaneously handling customs, storing royal merchandise, and providing living quarters for the Crown's officials in the only royal building Porto possessed.
Go inside and find the archaeological section in the lower floor.
In the nineteen nineties, when the building was being converted into its current use as a museum and municipal archive, excavations beneath the floor uncovered fourth and fifth-century Roman mosaic flooring and building foundations — the remains of the villa that Afonso IV's builders had simply built on top of seven hundred years earlier — and these are displayed in situ, under glass, so you can stand in a fourteenth-century customs house and look down through the floor at the Roman city underneath it, which I find one of the more quietly extraordinary things in Porto.
[beat]
The name Casa do Infante — House of the Prince — comes from an oral tradition, rather than a documented record, that Henry the Navigator was born here on the fourth of March, thirteen ninety-four, when his parents, King João the First and Queen Philippa of Lancaster, were in Porto and this customs house was the only royal building in the city and therefore the only plausible location for a royal birth to occur. Henry went on to sponsor the Portuguese expeditions that opened the Atlantic and fundamentally changed the world's geography — and the building where he was most likely born spent the following five centuries as a warehouse and tax office, which is the kind of biographical footnote that tends to be left out of the heroic versions of the story.

tours.title: Castelo do Queijo
tours.shortDescription: Two and three-quarter minutes at the 'Cheese Castle' — a 1661 trapezoidal granite fort built during the Restoration War on a boulder the locals thought looked like a wheel of cheese, with cannons on the parapet and pensioners playing cards at the base.
tours.longDescription:
The fort has a perfectly good official name — Forte de São Francisco Xavier — and absolutely no one uses it. The rock the fort sits on is the reason for the name everyone actually uses: a large, rounded granite boulder whose shape, to the eyes of the people who built here, resembled a wheel of cheese. Castelo do Queijo — Cheese Castle — is what it has been called ever since, with the official dedication to São Francisco Xavier quietly fading into administrative memory.

The trapezoidal granite structure was completed in 1661, designed by the engineer Miguel l'Ecole, and built not against dairy-related threats but during the Portuguese Restoration War — the period when Portugal was disentangling itself from sixty years of Spanish rule that had begun in 1580 — when the coastal defences along the Douro estuary were being systematically reinforced against the Spanish fleet. The walls are battered (sloped) for greater resistance to cannon fire, producing the characteristic low silhouette that makes the fort read as part of the rock rather than something placed on top of it. The arched entrance bears the carved coat of arms of a noble family; inside there are cannons on the parapet, a small exhibition about Portugal's Commando Association who currently maintain the building, and from the top a view that takes in the Atlantic, the beach below, and on clear days the coastline north toward Matosinhos and south toward the Douro mouth.

The fort had a turbulent nineteenth century: during the 1832 siege of Porto — the same Liberal Wars that burned the Convent of São Francisco in the Ribeira — it endured sustained bombardment, was plundered, and was left derelict for decades before settling into its current role as a monument. What the official history tends not to mention is that at dusk local retirees regularly gather at the base of the fort and use the battle-worn rocks as improvised tables and chairs for card games — which is the most honest measure of how completely Castelo do Queijo has been absorbed into the city it was once built to defend.

stops.title: Castelo do Queijo
stops.caption: At Praça de Gonçalves Zarco — the trapezoidal granite fort on its cheese-shaped boulder where the city meets the Atlantic.
stops.transcriptText:
The fort has a perfectly good official name — Forte de São Francisco Xavier — and absolutely no one uses it.
Right. You should be at the fort itself, or approaching it from Praça de Gonçalves Zarco, the roundabout at the western edge of Porto where the city meets the Atlantic — with the fort on its granite outcrop ahead of you, the beach to one side, and the ocean beyond. The rock the fort sits on is the reason for the name everyone actually uses: it's a large, rounded granite boulder whose shape, to the eyes of the people who built here, resembled a wheel of cheese, and so Castelo do Queijo — Cheese Castle — is what it has been called ever since, with the official dedication to São Francisco Xavier quietly fading into administrative memory.
Now look at the fort itself before you decide whether to go in.
The trapezoidal granite structure was completed in sixteen sixty-one, designed by the engineer Miguel l'Ecole, and it was built not — as the name might suggest — to guard against dairy-related threats, but during the Portuguese Restoration War, the period when Portugal was fighting to disentangle itself from sixty years of Spanish rule that had begun in fifteen eighty, and the coastal defences along the Douro estuary were being systematically reinforced against the Spanish fleet. The walls are built in a sloping manner — battered, in the technical term — which gives them greater resistance to cannon fire and produces the characteristic low, wide silhouette that makes the fort read as part of the rock rather than something placed on top of it.
The arched entrance gate bears the carved coat of arms of a noble Portuguese family, and inside there are cannons on the parapet, a small exhibition about Portugal's Commando Association who currently maintain the building, and from the top a view that takes in the Atlantic, the beach below, and on clear days the coastline running north toward Matosinhos and south toward the Douro mouth.
[beat]
The fort had a turbulent nineteenth century: during the eighteen thirty-two siege of Porto — the same Liberal Wars that burned the Convent of São Francisco in the Ribeira — it endured sustained bombardment and was subsequently plundered and left derelict for decades, passing through several owners and uses before settling into its current role as a monument. What the building's official history tends not to mention, but what I find the most honest measure of how a place has been absorbed into a city, is that at dusk local retirees regularly gather at the base of the fort and use the battle-worn rocks as improvised tables and chairs for card games — which suggests that Castelo do Queijo has achieved something that most military fortifications never manage, which is to become genuinely domestic.

tours.title: Igreja de Santo António dos Congregados
tours.shortDescription: Three and a quarter minutes at the Baroque church across from São Bento — same Jorge Colaço azulejo facade as the station opposite, plus the mummified body of Pope Saint Clement I in a side chapel, the only pope buried outside the Vatican.
tours.longDescription:
On the corner of Rua de Sá da Bandeira and Praça de Almeida Garrett, with the church's azulejo facade in front of you and São Bento Station across the square behind, the competition for attention is genuinely unequal: São Bento has twenty thousand tiles and trains; this church has a sober Baroque facade, its own twenty thousand tiles, and a papal relic in a side chapel that almost no visitor ever finds. The building has been here since 1703 — predating the Avenida dos Aliados, the station, and most of what surrounds it.

The blue-and-white azulejo panels covering the exterior were designed by Jorge Colaço — the same artist responsible for the twenty thousand tiles inside São Bento Station directly opposite, a detail that tends not to appear in the descriptions of either building. The panels depict scenes from the life of Saint Anthony and were applied in 1922 to a Baroque structure that dates from 1703 (built on the site of an earlier 1662 chapel), so the tiles are considerably younger than the walls they cover, as is the case with most of Porto's famous azulejo facades.

In a glass reliquary in the chapel of the Sagrada Família, on the right side of the nave, lies the mummified body of Pope Saint Clement I — the fourth Bishop of Rome and the first pope to be martyred, who died in the first century AD. According to a tradition of considerable antiquity, his remains were discovered by Saints Cyril and Methodius in Crimea in the ninth century and subsequently distributed among the churches of Europe; how part of them came to rest in this particular church in Porto is a matter the church's own documentation handles with appropriate scholarly caution, but the practical consequence is that this is the only place outside the Vatican where a pope is buried. The interior also has gilded Baroque pulpits, eighteenth-century lateral altarpieces, murals by Acácio Lino completed in 1927, and stained glass by Robert Léone from 1920 — three artists, three media, three centuries, all visible once you're inside.

stops.title: Igreja de Santo António dos Congregados
